Energy sector outlook In its latest monthly report for February 2026, Oil Market Report, the International Energy Agency (IEA) estimates an increase in global oil demand in 2026 of 0.85 mbl/d, to 104.9 mbl/d.
After European OECD countries maintained their consumption in 2025 compared with the previous year, the IEA estimates a drop of just 70 kbl/d in 2026.
The IEA notes that Chinese demand looks set to grow by 220 kbl/d in 2026, having increased its consumption by just 210 kbl/d in 2025.
In fact, the stagnation in Chinese oil demand growth is one of the reasons leading the IEA to project an oversupplied market in 2026.

The other reason for this scenario is the health of non-OPEC supply, which in 2026 is expected to grow by 1.17 mbl/ d, an increase similar to that expected for OPEC crude oil and natural gas liquids (+1.22 mbl/d), placing global supply at 108.6 mbl/d, which would leave the global balance at an oversupply of around 3.68 mbl/d on average in 2026, the highest level in the agency's historical records (going back to 1986).
In addition, the cyclical boost from AI and public spending on defense and energy transition could sustain inflationary pressures for longer than expected.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices In the longer term, the IEA notes in its 2025 World Energy Outlook document that geopolitical tensions, climate risks and the vulnerability of the energy system are placing energy at the center of economic and national security.
It also states that, despite global fragility, oil prices remain contained due to a large supply surplus.
Countries are seeking to strengthen their energy security in different ways: While some are focused on renewables and efficiency, others are prioritizing an abundant supply of traditional fuels.
At the same time, energy trade is becoming even more important in a context of geopolitical fractures and high uncertainty about international trade.
The IEA notes that global energy demand continues to grow strongly.
In 2024, both renewable energies and the consumption of oil, gas, coal and nuclear production reached record levels.
China has especially boosted the use of coal since 2019, contributing to the continued rise in emissions.

Thus, according to the scenarios published in the WEO 2025 by the IEA, under the STEPS baseline scenario, total global primary energy demand increases from around 15,623 million tons of oil equivalent (Mtoe) in 2024 to 17,839 Mtoe in 2050.
This corresponds to an average annual growth rate of 0.5%, just under half the growth rate of energy demand over the past decade.
Under the STEPS scenario, demand for each of the major fossil fuels—coal, oil and natural gas—would peak before 2035, retreating from that point forward.
Accordingly, in 2035 oil demand would stand at 100.4 mbl/d, while natural gas demand would reach 4,770 billion cubic meters (bcm).
All renewable energy sources increase their share over time.

At present, modern bioenergy (renewable fuels, liquid biofuels and solid bioenergy, excluding the traditional use of biomass) accounts for more than half of renewable energy demand, though solar photovoltaic and wind energy will expand rapidly over the coming years, especially in the electricity sector.
Installed capacity across all renewable energy sources will more than double under the STEPS scenario.
STEPS forecasts a peak in energy-related CO2 emissions before 2035, and they would fall from 38.15 Gt CO2 in 2024 to 29.63 Gt CO2 in 2050.
This would bring the global average temperature increase to approximately 2.4 °C by 2100.
In this edition, the IEA reintroduces the Current Policies Scenario (CPS) and abandons the Announced Pledges Scenario (APS).

In the CPS, which considers only the policies that have been adopted in legislation and actually implemented so far, global energy demand would reach 20,012 Mtoe by 2050, almost 15% above the level reached under the STEPS scenario.
In this scenario, oil demand would not fall throughout the analysis period, reaching 112.8 mbl/d by 2050.
The same is true of natural gas, which would reach 5,596 bcm.

7.2 Potential business developments In February 2024, Repsol presented its Strategic Update for the 2024–2027 period, which focuses on a profitable energy transition and prioritizes shareholder remuneration, underpinned by three pillars: value creation, maintaining a strong financial position, and investment discipline.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices The Upstream division will aim to focus on the execution of key projects that will improve production levels during 2026 (Pikka in Alaska, León/Castile in the Gulf of Mexico and Lapa SW in Brazil) and looking further ahead (Campos 33 in Brazil).

Progress will be made in digital transformation (driven by levers such as artificial intelligence and its generative aspect), specific programs will enhance the attraction, retention and development of talent, and cutting-edge technological solutions that support the evolution of businesses will continue to be deployed, with a focus on low-emission technologies.

• On the 4th of february of, 2026, after the conditions precedent set out in the agreement were met, the sale to Stonepeak of the 43.8% interest in a portfolio of 629 MW operating solar assets in Texas for 253 million dollars was closed, see Note 19 to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.. combining traditional activities with new low-emissions solutions.

The allocation of capital will be carried out rigorously, prioritizing the most profitable and transformative projects in the portfolio, with the aim of making solid progress on the transformation path toward decarbonization.
In the Customer area, efforts will aim to focus on improving profitability and cash generation, leveraged on a multi-energy proposal that adds value to the customer.

Repsol will aim to promote growth in electricity and natural gas customers, as well as the development of multi-energy and low-emission platforms , while promoting new businesses and the traditional business.

Within this framework, our points of sale will continue to be transformed and the distribution and marketing of renewable fuels will continue to be promoted, as a sustainable solution to face the challenge of decarbonization of transport.
In addition, we will accompany our customers in their energy transition, offering solutions that balance competitiveness, service and sustainability.

The Industrial businesses will aim to continue to drive transformation and the circular economy, through the development of initiatives aimed at increasing the capacity to produce renewable fuels and low-carbon materials, always promoting a culture of safety.

Among the vectors of progress, the start-up in 2026 of the advanced biofuels plant in Puertollano, which will produce 100% renewable diesel from waste, and the e- fuels plant in Bilbao, aimed at manufacturing synthetic fuels using green hydrogen and captured CO₂, stand out.
In addition, the expansion of the Sines petrochemical complex in Portugal will strengthen the production of polymeric materials.

On the other hand, after the approval of the projects in 2025, the construction of the Ecoplant in Tarragona, a pioneer in Europe in converting urban waste into renewable methanol through gasification, and the electrolyzer in Cartagena, which will allow the production of renewable hydrogen, will begin.

All this will be complemented by the consolidation of the production of the renewable fuels plant in Cartagena, operational since 2024, and with electrification and energy efficiency projects to decarbonize industrial processes.
The Low Carbon Generation business will continue to play a crucial role, with the organic development of a portfolio of solar and wind projects.
The new generation capacity will be especially focused on Spain and the United States.

The Repsol Group has established a system of Internal Control over Sustainability Reporting (“ICSR”), designed to provide reasonable assurance regarding the reliability of the sustainability information reported in accordance with the applicable regulatory framework, CNMV regulations, the Spanish Companies Act, and the Code of Good Governance of Listed Companies issued by the of the Spanish National Securities Market Commission (see details in Appendix IV, section 1.2 “Governance”).

Repsol has an Integrated Internal Control model, inspired by the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations of the Treadway Commission (COSO) reference framework, which includes, among others, the Group's formally developed Internal Control and Compliance Systems, including the Financial Reporting Internal Control System, the Sustainability Information Control System, the Crime Prevention Model and other compliance and criminal prevention models in force.
The price of CO2 emission allowances may be affected by the macroeconomic and regulatory environment, as well as the emission reduction scenarios associated with the energy transition process.
In 2025, the price of a barrel of Brent averaged $ 69.1 /bbl, lower than that of the previous year (80.8 $/ bbl).
The price of U.S. Henry Hub natural gas averaged 3.43 €/MBtu, higher than that of the previous period (2024: 2.27 $/MBtu).

In the case of electricity, the wholesale price averaged 66 €/MWh, compared with 63 €/MWh in 2024 , influenced by growth in demand that is offset by the increase in self- consumption and photovoltaic generation.
The price of CO2 emission allowances on the ETS1 market averaged 74.9 €/t compared with 66.5 €/t in 2024, mainly due to the increase in the price of gas in Europe.

The reduction in crude oil and gas prices has a negative impact on the profitability of the Exploration and Production activity, the valuation of its assets, the ability to generate cash and investment plans.
A potential drop in investment could adversely affect Repsol’s ability to replace its crude oil and gas reserves.
In addition, changes in the prices of crude oil and its derivatives may affect the margin of the Refining and Chemical businesses and the value of the inventories stored in the Industrial segment.

The price of gas and electricity has a fundamental impact on the low-carbon generation business and on the electricity and gas marketing businesses and the price of CO2 emission allowances in the ETS1 market on the margins of industrial businesses.
Likewise, the price of CO2 in the future ETS2 market may affect the margins and sales of commercial businesses.
For more information on the trend in the price of hydrocarbons, derivative products, and other commodities in 2025, see section 3.2 of this report.

Repsol’s industrial and commercial assets (refineries, petrochemical complexes, regasification plants, electric power generation plants (cogeneration, combined cycles, hydroelectric plants, wind or photovoltaic installations), bases and warehouses, port facilities, pipelines, ships, tankers, service stations, etc.
), as well as the facilities of Repsol's Upstream business (exploratory or production wells, surface facilities, oil platforms, etc.

Deviations when undertaking investment projects The project performance phase is exposed to various risks, such as geological, technical, economic, commercial, legal and regulatory, in addition to those related to safety, environment and sustainability, all of which can compromise the delivery of goods and services, compliance with pre-agreed budgets and deadlines, and compliance with defined specifications and/or operational reliability of projects.

Specifically, part of the processing, transportation and marketing of crude oil and gas production from Upstream assets is carried out through infrastructure (pipelines, processing and purification units or liquefaction terminals) operated by third parties.

In addition, there may also be restrictions on the supply of certain raw materials and/or equipment, in particular those linked to low-carbon electricity generation technologies and the production of renewable fuels.
For more information, see Note 27 “Geopolitical risks” to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.
During 2025, there were several power supply interruptions affecting Repsol's industrial assets for reasons beyond the Company’s control.
On April 22, the Cartagena refinery suffered a general blackout due to voltage instabilities in the grid, which disrupted the operation of the complex for five days.

Subsequently, on April 28, the mass blackout that struck the Iberian Peninsula and disconnected it from the continental electricity system caused the total shutdown of all Repsol's refineries and chemical complexes in Spain and Portugal.
Lastly, on June 16, the Puertollano refinery experienced another blackout due to a breakdown at a substation of the electricity supplier.

Board skills matrix Sustainability is among the competencies identified, understood as the ability to contribute to the sustainable development of the Company, taking into account the economic, social and environmental impacts of its activities and the expectations of its stakeholders.
This competence entails a focus on the principles of corporate social responsibility and to the environmental, social, and governance (ESG) criteria that govern the energy sector.

To identify directors who meet this competence in terms of sustainability, the following aspects have been considered: • Knowledge of the main plans and policies of other companies in the sector: experience in monitoring corporate policies, updating on matters of reputational significance and knowledge in corporate social responsibility and ESG.
• Knowledge of the relevant regulations related to ESG matters.
• Membership of the sustainability committees on the Boards of Directors of other large companies, especially in the field of energy.
• Knowledge of environmental, social (safety, human rights, labor aspects) and governance (ethics, compliance and business conduct) matters.
Remit over matters of sustainability encompasses the different environmental, social, and governance aspects, and therefore it relates to the Company’s material impacts, risks and opportunities.

Among them are the Chairman of Naturgy, environmental and safety experts who participate in ESG and sustainability committees, senior executives from Petronor, Sacyr and Petrobras, and other professionals who have advised governments and international regulators on energy and sustainable development affairs.

• Performance in natural capital and biodiversity, as well as in water management.
• Strategic environmental and safety projects.
• Performance in community relations and human rights and its contribution to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

The Sustainability Committee and the Executive Committee regularly monitor information on energy transition objectives, risk management, and the degree of fulfillment of corporate goals in environmental, social, and governance matters, as well as other strategic issues related to sustainability.
Specifically, the Sustainability Committee met five times in 2025 and reviewed, among other matters: • Fulfillment of the energy transition targets by the end of 2024.
• The consolidated map of the Group’s sustainability risks at the end of 2024 according to material ESG matters.
• The energy transition and climate change risks to which the Company is exposed in the 2030 and 2050 horizons.
• Review of advances in digitalization in the field of sustainability.
• The ratings of the main ESG analysts and rankings (ESG performance).
• The Global Sustainability Plan (closing of the 2024 Plan, and the 2025 Plan) and the Local Sustainability Plans.
• The 2024 Sustainable Development Goals Contribution Report.
• Progress in the circular economy strategy.
• Proposal and monitoring of the Company’s sustainability objectives (2024 Closing and 2025 Proposal).
• Updating of the energy transition and decarbonization metrics and targets.
• The reporting frameworks and regulatory framework for the sustainability information of the Consolidated Management Report.
• The process and results of the materiality assessment (impacts, risks, opportunities, and environmental dependencies8).
• Progress on environmental issues: circular economy, biodiversity and water management.
• The main implications for the Company of the COP30 agreements.
• Analysis of the content of the World Energy Outlook 2025 (WEO 2025).

7 The composition, functions, frequency of its meetings and other aspects related to the functioning of the Sustainability Committee are regulated in Article 37 of the Regulations of the Board of Directors.

Throughout 2025, issues were jointly presented to the Sustainability Committee and the Audit and Control Committee that, in accordance with corporate governance best practices, must be reviewed by both bodies.

In this regard, the two committees were briefed jointly on the following matters: (i) short- and long-term risk map of the Group; (ii) consolidated map of the Group’s sustainability risks at the end of 2024 according to material ESG matters; (iii) energy transition and climate change risks to which the Company is exposed in the 2030 and 2050 horizons; (iv) reporting and regulatory frameworks of the Consolidated Management Report; and (v) the map of emerging and climate change risks.

The Executive Committee is responsible for implementing the Strategic Plan and, in the field of sustainability, has among its functions: • Overseeing the Company’s policies, including those of sustainability, safety, the environment and human rights.
• Supporting the progress of and defining the Global Sustainability Plan and the Safety Excellence Plan.
• Overseeing and proposing to the Board of Directors the Company’s strategy in alignment with the energy transition, as well as analyzing long-term scenarios (2031-2050).
• Approving and evaluating objectives, including those of sustainability, environment and human rights, annual budgets and investment plans.
• Approving, monitoring and evaluating objectives, including those of sustainability, which are linked to the variable remuneration of the Company’s people.
• Approving the investment qualification methodology for its alignment with the energy transition and the evaluation of investment proposals and their impact on the CII.
• Approving any changes in decarbonization metrics and the monitoring, at least once a year, of progress towards achieving the energy transition and decarbonization targets.

Sustainability Coordination Committee In 2025, Repsol has defined a new body in charge of coordinating, facilitating and aligning ESG matters between the different businesses and expert areas in environmental, social, and governance matters with the aim of integrating sustainability into the Company's operations and achieving more agile management of material impacts, risks and opportunities.

Integration of sustainability-related performance in incentive schemes (GOV-3) To help ensure alignment across the organization, employee remuneration is reviewed annually by the Executive Committee, the Compensation Committee and the Board of Directors, the body that approves it, and is linked to compliance with the sustainability and decarbonization targets.
In 2025, targets related to environmental, social, and governance (ESG) matters were integrated into all the targets of each business unit, with a weight of between 20% and 45%.
In the particular case of the CEO, the weight of the targets related to decarbonization and sustainability represented 25% of their total variable annual remuneration in 2025.

In addition, there is a Long-Term Incentive (LTI) for the 2025–2028 period in which 40% of the targets are linked to the energy transition, which affects all management staff, including the CEO, and other employees.

Risk management and internal controls over sustainability reporting (GOV-5) The Repsol Group has been working in recent years, since the entry into force of Law 11/2018, to strengthen the Internal Control System over Sustainability Reporting (ICSR), the aim of which is to support the reliability of the sustainability information reported.

The ICSR is defined at Repsol following the same principles as the Internal Control System over Financial Reporting model and the guidelines of the COSO framework (mainly the COSO Integrated Framework and COSO ESG).

Procedures for reviewing and authorizing sustainability information and the description of the ICSR Repsol’s ICSR system aims to contribute to the reliability of the Company’s sustainability information reported in accordance with the current regulatory framework, as well as the regulation of the National Securities Market Commission (CNMV), the Capital Companies Act, and the Good Governance Code of Listed Companies of the Spanish National Securities Market Commission.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration This model of internal control over sustainability information is articulated through an integrated process that consists of five components developed through seventeen principles in accordance with the provisions of the COSO 2013 framework.

Governance model At the Sustainability Department (SD), there is a reporting unit on sustainability information that governs the sustainability information reported, under the supervision of the A&CC.

This unit: • Defines the criteria to be used in the reporting of sustainability information after analyzing the different regulatory frameworks in this field, and informs the A&CC of any significant changes in the reporting strategy.
• Prepares the sustainability reporting risk map in coordination with the unit that manages the integrated risk system and the economic- administrative unit.

• Defines procedures and/or guidelines that regulate, among other aspects, the roles and responsibilities of the areas involved in the sustainability information reporting process and the annual reporting milestone calendar.

The economic-administrative unit governs the financial information reported by the Company and is responsible for preparing, among other documents, the financial statements and the Group’s Management Report and, therefore, coordinates the reporting of the financial and sustainability information that must be presented publicly and so that it is disclosed in compliance with the deadlines and other requirements established by applicable regulations.

In addition, the economic-administrative unit and the SD define, in coordination with the business units and corporate areas affected, the processes and controls that support consistency between the financial and sustainability information presented in the Group’s financial statements and Management Report, as well as with other documents made public by Repsol.

Among other tasks, this unit oversees the evaluation of the design and operation of the controls of the Group’s Internal Control System over Sustainability Information, reports its results to the Audit and Control Committee and the Board of Directors of Repsol.
Model components The documentation making up the ICSR comprises the following components: • The sustainability reporting risk map (reviewed at least annually).
• Documentation of the processes within the scope of the ICSR.

The ICSR is articulated through a process in which, based on the identification and evaluation of the risks of reporting sustainability information, a scope model is defined that includes the companies covered, the set of relevant processes for preparation, the systems and the user-developed programs, the review and subsequent disclosure of sustainability information, and control activities aimed at the prevention and detection of any errors.
As a result of this process, the Repsol Group’s risk map is drawn up, of which sustainability risks are a part.

• Errors or omissions in the reporting of sustainability information.

Scope and updating The process of identifying and assessing the causal factors of sustainability reporting risks covers all the objectives of sustainability information, which could impact its reliability.
Control activities As indicated above, one of the components of the ICSR model is the control activities aimed at mitigating risks related to the reporting of sustainability information.

In this regard, the following types of controls have been identified in the ICSR: General controls • Entity-level controls: these operate throughout the Organization and allow us to achieve global control objectives, and cover the risk of “general environment” and comply with the COSO 2013 framework.

1.3 Business model and strategy Strategy, business model and value chain (SBM-1) Repsol is present throughout the energy value chain and markets a range of products in more than 90 countries.

The main products or services offered by the Company are detailed below: • Supply of crude oil, intermediate products, chemical raw materials and lipid residues consumed by the Group’s industrial complexes and third parties.
• Production and marketing of fuels, biofuels and petrochemical products in international markets.
The Company’s projects include the production of renewable hydrogen.
• Development, production and marketing of lubricants, aviation fuels, asphalts and petroleum-derived specialties.
• Marketing and trading of liquefied natural gas.
• Generation of low-emissions electricity in Spain, the USA, Chile and Italy.
• Marketing of retail Electricity and Gas in Spain and Portugal.

Customers of retail products and services are mainly consumers of fuels and other petroleum products (mainly marketed at service stations), natural gas, electric mobility, electricity and energy services.

The Company does not break down revenues by significant sector of the European Sustainability Standards (hereinafter, ESRS), or additional sectors of the ESRS, as the final list of sectors of the ESRS has not been effectively published by the EU.
There are no fossil gas revenues aligned with the technical criteria of the EU Sustainable Finance Taxonomy.
For more information, see Taxonomy Regulation.
(2) Relates mainly to gasoline, fuel oils, LPG, asphalts, lubricants, etc.
(3) Relates mainly to ethylene, propylene, polyolefins and intermediate products.
One of the key pillars of the Strategic Plan is the Company’s decarbonization, which mainly impacts issues of climate change and circular economy.
Social and governance issues support Repsol’s decarbonization plan.

Sustainability-related targets Repsol monitors the effectiveness of its policies and actions with respect to material sustainability-related IROs by defining their objectives in its Global Sustainability Plan.
The Global Sustainability Plan, which is approved by the Executive Committee and overseen by the Sustainability Committee, incorporates the main sustainability objectives.

This plan is the framework for action for their deployment at the local level in the different business units of countries and industrial centers of the Iberian Peninsula through the Local Sustainability Plans.
The Sustainability Committee of Repsol’s Board of Directors is responsible for monitoring and advising on policies and objectives in the areas of the environment, safety, and social responsibility.

In addition, it addresses voluntary processes, such as ISO 9001 quality certification, ISO 45001 safety, ISO 14001 environment, ISO 50001 energy management, and circular economy such as ISCC EU and OSC Europe.

Repsol is also evaluated by different ESG ratings (MSCI, Sustainalytics, CHRB, among others), which analyze its objectives and management practices, and have made a positive assessment of the Company’s performance and strategy.

Business model and value chain Repsol’s activities are structured into four segments: • Upstream: exploration and production of crude oil and natural gas reserves, as well as development of low-carbon geological solutions, especially CO2 storage.

• Industrial: petroleum refining, petrochemical and trading, transportation and wholesale marketing of crude oil, natural gas and fuels, including the development of activities related to new products such as hydrogen, renewable fuels and synthetic fuels.
• Customer: mobility (service stations) and marketing of fuels (gasoline, diesel, aviation kerosene, liquefied petroleum gases, biofuels, etc.
), electricity and gas and lubricants and other specialties.
• Low-Carbon Generation (LCG): generation of electricity from renewable sources (solar, wind, hydroelectric and storage) and through combined cycles of natural gas.
The Upstream business aims to develop and exploit the Company’s crude oil and gas reserves.
The main inputs of industrial complexes are crude oil, natural gas, intermediate materials, chemical raw materials and raw materials for the production of biofuels.
The trading activity aims to support the supply of these inputs to industrial complexes (including combined cycle plants) and the sale to third parties.

In relation to the aforementioned operations, the Company carries out Upstream activities for oil and gas, where it operates directly or in alliance with partners.
These activities may be supplemented with decarbonization initiatives involving carbon capture and storage.
Repsol transforms crude oil and various alternative raw materials, like residual plastic and other renewable material, through chemical and refining processes, and also produces renewable fuels.
These operations are integrated with the production and sale of electricity, an activity in which the Company participates both as a direct operator and through agreements with strategic partners.
The presence of these actors reflects an operating model based on cooperation and the shared development of energy solutions.

Finally, the marketing and sale of energy products and derivatives are managed through different channels, which include the distribution of LPG, petroleum derivatives, and the network of service stations where fossil fuels, renewables, and electricity are primarily sold.
In 2025, the value chain for energy companies in the O&G sector has been taken as a reference, which has been adapted to Repsol’s specific circumstances and processes.

Environmental information 2.1 Climate change 2.1.1 Governance Integration of sustainability-related performance in incentive schemes (ESRS 2 GOV-3) Repsol has set targets and metrics in relation to decarbonization and sustainability, integrating them into its annual variable remuneration system.
More precisely, 25% of the CEO’s annual variable remuneration is tied to sustainability and decarbonization objectives, with 15% tied to the latter.

Within this percentage, 5% is allocated to the increase in the installed capacity of renewable generation envisaged in the 2025 Annual Budget and 5% to the development of other low-carbon platforms and 5% to the reduction of the Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)1.
The details can be found in Targets and metrics set out in the CEO's 2025 Annual Variable Remuneration in relation to sustainability.

In addition, Repsol has implemented a Long-Term Incentive (LTI) for the period 2025-2028, which affects all management staff, including the CEO, and other employees, in which 40% of the objectives are tied to the energy transition.
This LTI includes specific metrics such as the reduction of the CII, renewable generation capacity (GW), and renewable fuel production capacity (Mt/a).
2.1.2 Strategy The energy sector faces the challenge of supplying secure and affordable energy in a context of growing energy demand, driven global economic growth.
At the same time, energy efficiency and a transition to decarbonized energies are needed in order to meet the urgency of curbing the impact of greenhouse gas emissions on the climate.
The pace of this energy transition is mainly determined by technological development and the implementation of enabling policies on a global and regional scale.

Transition plan for climate change mitigation (E1-1) In 2019, Repsol began its transformation to align itself with an energy transition compatible with the global ambition to achieve net zero emissions.

Since then, it has made this commitment concrete through different strategic plans, the most recent being 2024–2027 Strategic Update approved by the Board of Directors on February 21, 2024, which reinforces the Company’s decarbonization objectives.

This strategic process was completed with the presentation of the Energy Transition Plan and its submission to an advisory vote at the Annual General Meeting on May 10, 2024, in which it obtained the support of 70% of the capital present or represented.
Transition metrics and targets are regularly reviewed to adapt them, where necessary, to the environment in which energy sector operates.

Repsol reaffirms its focus on climate change mitigation and its ambition to reach net zero GHG emissions by 2050, in line with the Paris Agreement, which seeks to limit global warming to 1.5°C and achieve climate neutrality by the second half of the century2.

Repsol’s energy transition strategy enables the Company to progressively decarbonize its own activities (reducing its Scope 1 and 2 emissions) while also contributing to the decarbonization of the value chain, especially for consumers, through the development of low- or zero-carbon3 products (Scope 3, category 11).
In this context, Repsol set the goal of achieving net zero emissions by 2050, which involves reducing greenhouse gas emissions to net zero in Scopes 1, 2 and 3.

To make progress towards this goal, the Company set two targets for 2030: reducing direct emissions by 55% (Scope 1 and 2 under operational control) compared to 2016 and reducing total emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3) by 20% compared with 2018.

Scopes 1 and 2 include emissions from operated assets and Scope 3 the emissions associated with category 11 for the use of the products sold (gas and petroleum products).4 1 Under Repsol's criteria, low-carbon investments include all investments in projects that contribute to the Company's decarbonization, and refer to projects that do not generate emissions or do so in a very low proportion compared to their fossil energy alternatives.
It does not fully correspond to the European Taxonomy's definition of sustainable investments.

2 The goal of achieving carbon neutrality is to achieve a balance in the second half of the century between anthropogenic emissions by sources and anthropogenic removals by sinks, on the basis of equity and in the context of sustainable development and efforts to eradicate poverty, as set out in Article 4.1 of the Paris Agreement.
The Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics provides detailed information on the analysis used to determine the strategy’s alignment with the 1.5°C objective.
3 Low- or zero-carbon products refer to those that generate low or no greenhouse gas emissions throughout their life cycle, compared to conventional products.
4 In this metric, Scope 3 refers to Category 11 (use of sold products).
The absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions metric covers 91% of the Company’s total operated emissions.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Since the energy transition requires reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions while simultaneously meeting society’s energy demand, the Company also uses the CII as a key metric to assess progress towards decarbonization per unit of primary energy consumption.
This indicator reflects the combined impact of the main pillars of decarbonization, which are key to its climate strategy, namely the development of electricity and renewable fuels.
Repsol has set targets to reduce its Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)5 by 15% for 2025 compared to 2016: 25% ±1% for 2030, 55% for 2040 and 100% for 2050.
Repsol thus has two complementary indicators that incorporate Scope 3 emissions with different perimeters and that strengthen its decarbonization strategy.

It is noteworthy that by 2025 all the established decarbonization targets have been achieved: a) reducing the CII by 15%; b) reducing methane intensity to 0.20% in the operated assets of Upstream; and c) reducing emissions from routine gas flaring by 50% in the same assets compared to 2018.
These last two targets contribute to the 2030 Scope 1 and 2 emissions reduction target.

Decarbonization pillars and levers Repsol’s energy transition strategy is structured around five strategic pillars which represent the main areas of action in the energy transition towards net zero emissions.
For each pillar, decarbonization levers are deployed, which are concrete initiatives that generate effective and quantifiable reductions in Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions.
Each pillar integrates both these levers and other mainly commercial actions that, although they do not directly reduce emissions, support the transition in the Company’s energy model.

This structure makes it possible to clearly distinguish between the strategic framework (pillars) and the operational mechanisms (levers) that drive the achievement of the 2030 interim targets, as set out in Actions and resources in relation to climate change policies (E1-3) and Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).

Operational excellence and asset portfolio management Energy efficiency, electrification of processes, reduction of direct CO2 and methane emissions from current operations and optimization of Upstream asset portfolio towards less carbon-intensive assets are the levers for achieving the target of a 55% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 2030 compared with the 2016 baseline year.
Capital allocation to the Upstream business is consistent with stable production levels in the range of 550–600 kboe/d throughout this decade.

As previously indicated, investment is prioritized in higher‑margin assets and projects with lower greenhouse gas emissions, which will enable a reduction in carbon intensity over the coming years, reaching an intensity of 10 kgCO2e/boe in 2027.
At its industrial businesses (Refining and Chemicals), the Company plans to continue implementing measures, electrification of processes, and increasing the use of renewable electricity.
Renewable electricity generation Renewable generation is key to Repsol’s energy transition to reduce emissions per unit of energy produced.

Since the start of this activity in 2018, Repsol has built a portfolio of renewable electricity projects (wind, solar and hydro), with 5.8 GW of installed capacity in 2025 and projected to exceed 106 GW by 2030, especially in Spain and the USA.

Growth in the coming years has been adjusted to developments in the environment, marked by higher development and financing costs, as well as lower tax incentives in the United States, prioritizing investments according to the profitability thresholds established by Repsol.

Liquid and gaseous renewable fuels The competitiveness of Repsol’s refineries, in the top European quartile by economic7 margin, enables their transformation, in alignment with demand trends promoted by regulation and focusing on the circular economy, with the use of organic waste to produce renewable fuels and the recycling of plastics.

The refineries will continue to operate through to 2030, due to the resilience shown by demand in Spain and the selective expansion to adjacent markets, while increasing the production of renewable fuels.
5 The CII includes in its numerator the Scope 1 and 2 emissions operated, as well as the Scope 3 emissions of categories 11, 12 and 1 related to the primary energy produced by the Company.
These categories account for approximately 37% of total Scope 3 emissions.
Together, the CII covers 41% of the Company’s total operated emissions.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Repsol plans to reach a production capacity6 in Europe by 2030 of 1.6-1.8 Mt of renewable fuels, 0.7-0.8 TWh of biomethane and 0.6-0.8 GWe of renewable hydrogen, with these metrics adjusted from 2024 projections to reflect demand and regulatory trends, particularly with regard to renewable hydrogen.
The targets for renewable fuel and biomethane production are detailed in Targets related to resource use and the circular economy (E5-3).
Multi-energy for customers Repsol is moving toward a multi-energy model focused on the supply from different energy sources to meet both the needs of its customers and European decarbonization goals.
This approach allows the Company to reduce emissions throughout its value chain (Scope 3) by diversifying its portfolio of energy products and services.

Repsol is a leading player in the liquid and gaseous fuels market in the Iberian Peninsula, and has expanded its offer to include renewable fuels, residential and mobility electricity, and other low-carbon products.

Repsol has developed in the Iberian Peninsula one of the most extensive 100% renewable fuel networks in Europe, with availability at more than 1,550 service stations, with 56% of the network in Spain offering multi-energy solutions that include 3,600 public charging points for electric vehicles.

The Company is also developing the sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) market and now has a 100% renewable fuel production plant at its Cartagena refinery, with a capacity of 250,000 tons per year for the co-production of renewable road diesel and SAF from oily waste.
In the field of residential energy marketing, Repsol has 3 million electricity and gas customers in Spain and Portugal, with the aim of reaching 5 million by 2030.
Development of carbon sinks Carbon sinks constitute a pillar that will not be material for the Company at least until after 2030.

Repsol’s decarbonization pathway considers the long‑term contribution of carbon sinks (CO₂ capture and storage projects and, eventually, natural solutions if needed) to complete net-zero GHG emissions by 2050).
By 2030, the Company's portfolio of marketed products will reflect a shift towards more decarbonized alternatives.

These will include renewable electricity and renewable fuels (liquid fuels, biogas and hydrogen), where 20% of the portfolio will consist of low-carbon products (based on energy content), together with a reduction in sales of natural gas and petroleum products, which are the levers associated with the Company's reduction of Scope 3 emissions.

% Product mix (based on energy content) 1 Low-carbon molecules correspond to products marketed by the Company, whose use does not generate net CO2 emissions such as biofuels, renewable hydrogen, synthetic fuels, chemical products, lubricants and asphalts.

Repsol assesses progress in the execution of its energy transition strategy through the various indicators detailed in sections E1-4 and E1-6, including absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions, operational emissions (Scopes 1 and 2) and the CII.
• In 2025, Repsol's total emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3, category 11)4 amounted to 184.7 MtCO₂e, down 17.4% on 2018, advancing toward the target of –20% by 2030.
• Scope 1 and 2 emissions stood at 13.9 MtCO₂e in 2025, down 45.3% on 2016 and on track to reach the -55% reduction commitment of these emissions for 2030.
• Meanwhile, the CII stood at 65.2 gCO₂e/MJ, which represents a decline of 15.1% compared with 2016, exceeding the target of –15% in 2025 and with a focus on achieving a reduction of 25% ±1 in 2030.

These results reflect sustained progress in the decarbonization of the Company's energy portfolio and the consolidation of its strategy to reach net zero emissions by 2050, as detailed in Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).

Investments Repsol has classified the following investments as low- carbon: energy efficiency and process electrification, renewable electricity generation, production and marketing of renewable fuels (biofuels, renewable hydrogen, synthetic fuels, biogas), chemicals (long-life polymers) and non-combustible products, CCS, marketing of renewable electricity, distributed generation and renewable electric mobility, and R&D and corporate venturing investments in low-carbon technologies.

Based on this methodology, investments are classified into three categories: • Aligned with the energy transition, if they do not negatively affect achievement of the targets for reduction of the CII or if they contribute to achieving these targets.
• Enabling the energy transition when they have a negative impact of less than 1% on the CII and this can be offset with other initiatives.

For Upstream, investments are subject to additional conditions, such as the limitation of the useful life of exploitable reserves and the exclusion of extra-heavy crudes, tar sands and offshore projects in the Arctic.
This approach allows for investment decisions that are aligned with the CII reduction target.

In 2025, using the investment qualification methodology, a report from the Sustainability Department was incorporated into 16 investment proposals (8 from Upstream, 5 from Low-Carbon Generation and 3 from Industrial).
To promote investment decisions consistent with its decarbonization goals, Repsol applies an internal carbon price to all projects.

1.5 ºC alignment Repsol has assessed its energy transition strategy against scenarios of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) that are compatible with limiting global warming to 1.5 °C by the end of this century.

In particular, the analysis is based on mitigation scenarios classified as C1, which represent global pathways with a probability greater than 50% of not exceeding this threshold by the end of the century, and with a transient and limited overshoot of temperature above 1.5 °C.
There are other scenarios, classified as C2, also compatible with 1.5 ºC, but involve a more prolonged temperature overshoot.
The C1 scenarios envisage a global reduction in greenhouse gas emissions of 40–50% in 2030 compared with 2019, with steeper declines in coal (≈ 75%) and smaller declines in oil and gas (≈ 10%).

Of the total of 97 IPCC C1 scenarios, those that achieve a net reduction of more than 95% of emissions from the energy sector by 2050 (19 scenarios) show a median reduction in greenhouse gas emissions from oil and gas of 14% in 2030.
Similar results are observed when restricting the analysis to the nine scenarios with assumptions that Repsol considers more realistic in terms of technological availability and carbon costs.

Meanwhile, the International Energy Agency’s (IEA) Net Zero Emissions (NZE) scenario, updated in the WEO 2025 and aligned with a limit of 1.5 °C13 and emissions neutrality by 2050, does not project a specific data for 2030.

By interpolation, a reduction of 22% in oil and gas is estimated for 2030 compared with 2019.14 From this analysis, it can be concluded that Repsol’s goal of achieving a 20% reduction in absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions by 2030 compared with 2018, which covers more than 91% of the Company’s total emissions, falls within the range of the set of IPCC and IEA scenarios compatible with limiting global warming to 1.5 °C by the end of this century and achieving net zero emissions by 2050.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Qualitative analysis of locked-in emissions Calculations of potential locked-in emissions associated with the main assets and products marketed by Repsol are based on an estimate of future GHG emissions over their useful life.

Oil and gas products could be subject to drastic demand reductions over the long term that would affect the value of the Company assets that produce them and could eventually require halting its activity, leaving the assets and their emissions “locked in”.

In its climate transition risks and resilience analysis, Repsol classifies the risk associated with potential locked-in emissions as significant and manages its portfolio of assets to strengthen its resilience to this risk.

The resilience of the assets for the production, transformation and sale of oil and gas products has been assessed, including both the emissions generated directly by those that are operated (Scope 1 and 2) and those derived from the use of the products sold (Scope 3).

The risk of locked-in emissions is taken into account when setting decarbonization targets: • With respect to assets, Scope 1 and 2 emissions from the facilities operated are part of the reduction trajectory detailed in Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).

• Regarding products, of the 20% reduction target for Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions compared with the 2018 baseline year, 17.4% of the reduction corresponds to emissions associated with the use of the products sold.

The analysis of the Company’s future emissions is classified by business: Upstream or Downstream: Upstream The Company’s strategy is focused on optimizing its portfolio of exploration and production assets, giving priority to those with higher profitability and lower carbon intensity, with a greater geographical concentration to gain scale.

Priority is given to projects for the development of discovered reserves and resources that are profitable at moderate oil and gas prices and exploration adjacent to existing assets and infrastructure, excluding new exploratory frontiers, tar sands and the Arctic offshore.
As a result, Scope 1 and 2 emissions have already been reduced, reaching an intensity that is now within the first quartile of the sector.

This intensity will continue to decline in the coming years, with projections of reaching 10 kgCO2e/boe for 2027.15 For the Upstream business, Scope 3 emissions derive from the use of hydrocarbon production.

From 2035, a gradual reduction is expected reaching around 100 kboed in 2050 due to the natural decline of assets without undertaking new long-term developments in scenarios compatible with the warming limited to 1.5 ºC.

Downstream Most of Repsol’s downstream assets are located in Spain and Portugal, where the European regulatory framework on climate matters applies, in line with the European Green Deal and its implementation through the Fit for 55 legislative package with emission reduction targets for 2030, aimed at achieving climate neutrality by 2050.

Within this framework, Repsol manages the locked-in emissions of the products sold (Scope 3, category 1116) by progressively reducing refining activity from 2030 and the gradual replacement of conventional products with low-carbon products such as renewable liquid and gaseous fuels and synthetic fuels.

The EU regulation also promotes the reduction of Scope 1 and 2 emissions through greater energy efficiency, the electrification of processes with renewable energies and, in the longer term, through the capture, use and storage of CO2, or the trend to reduce the processing of crude oil in refineries.

Material impacts, risks and opportunities and their interaction with strategy and the business model (ESRS 2 SBM-3) The resilience analysis carried out in 2025 covers all the Company’s own operations, in all segments of the value chain where it operates, and in its four business lines (Upstream, Industrial, Customer, and Low-Carbon Generation).

The Company’s ability to adapt to the progressive decarbonization of the energy system in different transition scenarios is assessed, promoting operational continuity, economic competitiveness and sustainable development in all business areas.
This study has as its starting point the analysis of transition and physical risks17 from the double materiality assessment.

However, given that this analysis concludes that exposure to physical risks derived from climate change is not material in any of the scenarios and time horizons analyzed, (as they do not generate significant impacts on the operational continuity or economic competitiveness of the Company’s key assets), these physical risks have not been included in the scope of the resilience analysis.
16 Scope 3 category 11 emissions account for 84% of the Company’s current total emissions.
17 Resilience analysis and the identification of transition risks are complementary but distinct exercises.

Although they are based on the same scenarios and assumptions, resilience analysis is a scenario analysis, so it does not assess each risk individually, but rather analyzes in an integrated manner the capacity for adaptation and value creation of the Company’s businesses under different rhythms of the energy transition, based on aggregate variables, such as demand, prices or margins, with an effect on the projection of economic-financial metrics.

The analysis covers the period between the present and 2050, in correspondence with the temporal scope of the climate risk analysis and the Company’s decarbonization objectives.
Three reference climate scenarios from the expert consultancy S&P Energy updated in 2025 have been selected, which are applied differently in each business as explained in the following paragraphs.

These scenarios, recognized in the sector, unlike other reference scenarios, contain production, demand and price projections of different energy sources and products with sufficient granularity at the level of geography, seasonality and raw material/product to allow for developing a more complete and consistent analysis.

The results of the analysis show the performance of the different businesses, as detailed below, as well as the Company’s resilience to risks arising from changes in the pace of the energy transition: • In an environment for Industrial and Customer businesses determined by the European Green Deal and the Fit for 55 legislative package, investments are envisaged in renewable fuel projects that preserve their economic value, where they are currently positioned in the first quartile within the EU and with the largest market share in Spain.

• Meanwhile, the Low-Carbon Generation and Electricity Distribution businesses increase their capacity to create value over time due to the growth and investment strategy proposed and the trend towards electrification in the scenarios posed.

The resilience analysis carried out, which projects the performance of businesses based on the assumptions of the scenarios used, shows that the proposed strategy is resilient in the event of changes in the pace of the energy transition.

As part of the resilience analysis, the capital allocation required under each of the long‑term scenarios considered has been estimated, as well as the proportion that should be directed to low‑carbon businesses.
The following material impacts have been identified in relation to climate change: • Repsol’s sale of the fossil fuels demanded by the market generates indirect emissions of CO2 (Scope 3) (IRO-9).

• Impact on global warming (IRO-4) of Repsol’s activities in the areas of Upstream and Industrial, as well as those related to mobility, which generate Scope 1 and 2 (direct company operations) and Scope 3 (mobility and supply chain) emissions.

• Repsol’s operating model involves an intensive use of energy from non-renewable sources, which contributes significantly to its direct greenhouse gases emissions (IRO-1) and can generate exposure to variations in energy costs and an increasingly demanding regulatory environment.

• Repsol’s leadership position in the market can serve as an example for other companies and institutions when adopting their energy transition measures (IRO-5), promoting a shift toward more sustainable operations and contributing to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions and to collective progress toward decarbonization.
• Commitment and awareness of the workforce in terms of decarbonization due to the link of their variable remuneration to this target (IRO-6).

• Repsol’s renewable energy services, from wind, solar and hydro sources, contribute positively to the energy market (IRO-2) by increasing the supply and consumption of renewable energy, thus favoring the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions and the move towards a low-carbon energy model.
Repsol assesses the physical risks of climate change and the risks of the energy transition separately through the methodologies described below.

Physical risk analysis of climate change In 2022, Repsol developed its own, semi-quantitative methodology for the detailed analysis of the physical risks of climate change, both in existing facilities and in investment projects subject to the Taxonomy Regulation.
First, an identification is made of the climatic phenomena to which Repsol’s assets are potentially exposed because they are located in certain geographical locations.
The following natural phenomena are included: 1.
Extreme wind (storms, tornadoes) 6.

The climate projections that have been used are, among others, those from Copernicus services (the EU’s Earth observation program, coordinated and managed by the European Commission) as well as external consultancy services that provide additional information on the behavior of these physical variables and their combinations.

In addition, account has been taken of the global warming scenarios described by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), with a wide range of emissions (SSP5-8.5, SSP2-4.5 and SSP1-2.6 scenarios) selected in the 2030, 2040 and 2050 time horizons.

This study, carried out in the absence of mitigation or adaptation measures, is first carried out on a representative sample of the assets in the current portfolio of all the Company’s businesses, which is then extrapolated to other assets that are compatible in location and typology.
In addition, more detailed analyses of assets subject to the European taxonomy regulation have been carried out.
The conclusion of these studies is that the increase in risk from acute or chronic climate change events is not material18 for the Repsol Group in any of the analyzed scenarios or time horizons.
Risk analysis of the energy transition Transition risks and opportunities are important, especially in the medium and long term, which is why Repsol reviews them on an annual basis.
The objective of this process is to identify, evaluate and make sure that these risks are known and taken into account for decision-making through resilience analysis and the Company’s strategy.
In the medium and long term, a specific analysis of the transition risks is carried out for each of the four major business areas.

These analyses are carried out taking as a reference the projections of the latest update of the Strategic Plan announced by the Company, as well as the decarbonization route, and as the environmental scenario the NZE scenario of S&P Energy20, aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5 ºC with limited overshoot, which enables the determination of the residual risk to which the Company would be exposed in these horizons and scenarios.

Policies related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-2) The management of the impacts, risks, and opportunities arising from climate change is included in the Sustainability Policy, which establishes as priority objectives the promotion of a fair energy transition that takes into account its impact on workers, local communities and society in general while reducing and avoiding greenhouse gas emissions, and the transition to low-carbon business models that reduce or mitigate the adverse effects of climate change and integrate sustainability into all businesses.
In this policy, Repsol also takes on the challenge of contributing to universal access to safe, affordable and sustainable energy.
Accordingly, it emphasizes that all reduction technologies will be necessary to achieve this goal, so that the energy transition is carried out in an orderly manner and at the lowest cost to society.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration which underscores the need to take account of stakeholder expectations and to “provide energy and products to society to meet its needs efficiently, guaranteeing adequate protection of the environment and the sustainable use of resources without compromising the well-being of future generations”.

In this policy, the Company commits to identifying, evaluating, and managing, in accordance with the mitigation22 hierarchy, the impacts, dependencies, risks, and opportunities on the environment, in the activities and places where Repsol operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services offered to society.
Repsol is also committed to assessing the risks associated with climate change and implementing climate change mitigation and adaptation measures in its operations when such risks are material.

The Environmental Policy also includes Repsol’s commitment to integrate the principles of the circular economy into its processes, products, and services, optimizing the use of resources and raw materials, including energy; encouraging the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained in a sustainable way; and promoting the development of new products with less environmental impact.

In addition, Repsol undertakes to promote environmental commitments in accordance with those defined internally throughout the value chain, customers, partners, suppliers of products and services, and other stakeholders.

The commitments included in this document, together with the Sustainability Policy, contribute to achieving the Company’s goal of net zero emissions by 2050, in line with the Paris Agreement, as well as the other decarbonization objectives specified in the transition plan.
The Sustainability Department ensures the implementation of the Sustainability and Environment policies.

Actions and resources in relation to climate change policies (E1-3) Below are the actions that Repsol has implemented in 2025 to mitigate climate change and its impact in 2025, along with forecasts for 2030, as well as the resources allocated for their execution.

The information covers different lines of action depending on the scope of the emissions (1, 2 and 3) and the nature of the measures, considering both current investments and future projections (see Investments).

Energy efficiency23 In the industrial field, which includes the complexes located in Spain, Portugal and Peru within the value chain downstream, measures are implemented such as the cleaning of heat exchangers, the optimization of energy integration, the use of heat from high-temperature streams, the improvement of furnace efficiency and the optimization of steam networks.
Further, at Upstream, which covers assets in various geographies within the upstream value chain, notable initiatives include reducing methane emissions and minimizing routine flaring of gas.

Use of renewables25 Renewable electricity is a lever for decarbonization of Scope 1 emissions, if it replaces electricity generation in cogeneration; and Scope 2, if it replaces the purchase of electricity on the market that continues to have a fossil component.

This substitution can be done through on- site generation in self-consumption facilities (solar photovoltaic and wind), or through renewable energy purchase agreements (PPAs), which guarantee supply and offer price stability.

In October 2023, the Company put into operation a 2.5 MW electrolyzer at the Petronor industrial complex (Bilbao), intended for the production of renewable hydrogen for industrial use by third parties, and therefore this hydrogen is neither used nor consumed at Repsol’s facilities.

The 10 MW electrolyzer in Bilbao, associated with the synthetic fuels demonstration plant, is currently in the execution phase, as well as the 4 MW electrolyzer in Sines (Portugal), which is expected to come into operation in 2026.

These include the 100 MW electrolyzers in Cartagena and Bilbao, which are expected to reach the final investment decision (FID) in 2025, and are recognized as an Important Project of Common European Interest (IPCEI) and is scheduled to come into operation from 2029.

Carbon sinks27 CO₂ capture and storage is a technological lever for the decarbonization of industrial and Exploration and Production assets, applicable when it is technically and economically feasible.

As part of Repsol’s medium- and long-term strategy, which already includes two projects under evaluation in Spain and the United States, and aims to develop capabilities that would enable CO₂ storage, always subject to regulatory maturity and demand for storage services.

Scope 3 emissions Repsol’s Scope 3 emissions are mostly associated with the use of the energy products it markets (petroleum- based fuels and natural gas), so their reduction requires action on the volume and mix of sales, in line with energy transition scenarios and European regulation.

Within the framework of the Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions reduction target, the Company combines portfolio management decisions, evolution of the commercial offer and growth in lower carbon intensity solutions to progressively reduce emissions derived from the use of its products.

Sales of petroleum products28 Repsol’s oil products are marketed mainly in the Iberian Peninsula, operating within Repsol’s marketing of petroleum products is subject to the European Union’s policy and regulatory framework.
This regulatory environment sets the trend with respect to the evolution of the transport sector and the associated demand for fuels.

Nonetheless, this demand shows notable resilience in the Iberian Peninsula in the short term, with stable projections throughout this decade and point to a progressive decrease in the demand for conventional fossil fuels in the medium and long term, with the consequent increase in renewable fuels for all mobility and electric mobility, mainly in light road transport.

2.1.4 Metrics and targets Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4) In 2024, Repsol set a new absolute emissions reduction target for Scopes 1, 2 and 3 as part of its strategy to address climate risks and opportunities, thus further attesting to its alignment with the global targets of limiting warming to 1.5 °C and net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
This target is a response, to a large extent, to the demands of certain interest groups and the CSRD Directive.

Its approach is complementary to the CO2e emissions reduction target per unit of energy generated (carbon intensity) set in 2019, and to other specific targets for reducing Scope 1 and 2 emissions, methane and routine flare emissions.

The following describes the metrics that the Company uses to assess the progress of impacts, risks, and opportunities (IROs) related to the energy transition and decarbonization (see Materiality assessment).
All decarbonization goals are consistent with the Company’s business and operational projections, which are shared with stakeholders in their strategic updates.

In addition, they contribute directly to the objectives of the Sustainability Policy and the Environment Policy, directing actions towards optimizing the use of resources, improving energy efficiency and achieving climate neutrality.
(4) "Near Zero" methane intensity is defined as below 0.2%, according to the formulation identified by OGCI.

Absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions (MtCO2e)4,30 Repsol uses this metric31 of absolute greenhouse gas emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, to show its progress in decarbonization and as the form most accepted by certain stakeholders, in the absence of a recognized standard, to demonstrate its alignment with the global objectives of limiting warming to 1.5 °C and achieving net-zero GHG emissions by 2050, as detailed in Transition plan for climate change mitigation (E1-1).
By 2030, Repsol has set a goal to reduce absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 GHG emissions by 20%, compared with the 201832 baseline, with an estimated contribution of 5% in Scopes 1 and 2 and 15% in Scope 3.
Based on current projections, the Company estimates a reduction of more than 90% in absolute emissions with the mitigation levers and technologies currently envisaged.
The remaining percentage, around 10%, is expected to be addressed through greenhouse gas removal and abatement solutions, including carbon capture and storage technologies and natural sinks.

Carbon Intensity Indicator (gCO2e/MJ)24 In 2019, Repsol defined the CII in gCO2e/MJ.

This metric is calculated by adding Scope 1 and 2 (operational) and Scope 3 greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (based on products obtained from Repsol’s oil and gas production) in the numerator, and the energy generated by the Company in the denominator (see Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics).
In the CII numerator, approximately 37% of the Company’s Scope 3 emissions and 41% of total emissions are covered, as the Company’s oil product sales are higher than its oil production33.
The denominator of the CII represents the energy that Repsol makes available to society as final products derived from the production of primary energy from oil and gas and low-carbon energy sources.
By 2050, the goal is to reduce the carbon intensity of the primary energy we produce to net zero, with interim targets in 2025, 2030 and 2040.

The CII contributes directly to Sustainability and Environmental Policies by offering an integrated and quantifiable metric that guides the Company’s progress towards net-zero GHG emissions, along with the Scope 1, 2 and 3 absolute emissions metric.
This indicator reinforces the commitment to reduce GHG emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3), aligning with the principles of continuous improvement set out in the Environmental Policy.

In addition, the CII facilitates the strategic management of our energy transition by providing a tool to assess the impact of decarbonization actions that are not identifiable through the reduction of absolute emissions (renewable power generation, renewable fuels), as noted, which supports the Sustainability Policy by prioritizing the integration of the energy transition and climate resilience into the Company’s operations.
In October 2021, the CII reduction targets were set at 15% for 2025, 28% for 2030, 55% for 2040 and 100% for 205034, compared to the base year of 2016.

Over the last few months, and after meeting all of its decarbonization targets for 2025, Repsol has reviewed certain metrics and objectives from the latest strategic update (which are included in this report) in order to adapt them to the better understanding of the global energy context, regulation and demand trends, and the pace of the energy transition.
And it does so without departing from the path of decarbonization toward net- zero GHG emissions by 2050, and undertaking investments that are profitable in the current environment.
By 2030, as a result, the reduction target for the CII has been set at 25% ±1.
In 2025, the CII stood at 65.2 gCO₂e/MJ, representing a 15.1% reduction compared to 2016 (13.4% in 2024), thus meeting the 15% reduction target.
34 Net zero will be achieved by reducing numerator emissions by at least 90% (emissions covering 42% of the Company’s current total emissions) and neutralizing residual emissions.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration GHG removals and GHG mitigation projects financed through carbon credits (E1-7) Currently, the Company does not have any tons of CO2 stored that are derived from CO2 removal projects from the atmosphere implemented in its own operations or in which it has contributed in upstream and downstream stages of its value chain.

As part of its commitment to the goal of net zero emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, Repsol envisages that up to 8% of these emissions (around 20 MtCO2e) cannot be eliminated by 2050 through technologies applied to its energy39 production, in which case it would offset with its own natural climate solutions projects (NCS) or through credits.

These credits would be aligned with the most demanding integrity frameworks, such as Article 6 of the Paris Agreement and/or the European Union’s CRCF, guaranteeing transparency, additionality, permanence and methodological rigor.
In any case, Repsol’s decarbonization strategy prioritizes avoiding and reducing its own emissions and those in its value chain through technological development and operational efficiency.
At least until after 2030, Repsol will not use emission offset mechanisms.
Notwithstanding the foregoing, Repsol has implemented commercial initiatives that allow its customers to voluntarily contribute to the mitigation of their carbon emissions.

Although these credits finance projects that capture or avoid CO2 emissions, such as forestry projects, their use is not linked to Repsol’s40 global decarbonization strategy, but is offered as an additional and voluntary option for customers who wish to contribute to the mitigation of emissions that come from the use of our products.

In 2025, the Company has cancelled 20,340 tCO2 in the form of international carbon credits to cover the demand for these initiatives, entirely of the AFOLU, REDD+ type and verified by Verra’s41 VCS42 (Verified Carbon Standard), with additional CCB+ Gold certification43.
It should be noted that these credits are not used to offset GHG emissions under the E1-6 requirement, nor as a means to achieve the Company’s emissions reduction goals outlined in the E1-4.

Internal carbon pricing (E1-8) Repsol has internal mechanisms to promote the allocation of capital to low-carbon investments, such as the internal carbon price and the investment qualification methodology for their alignment with the energy transition.
The Company has established an internal carbon price for investment decisions for new projects.
The internal carbon price applies to the analysis of new investments on the potential impact of the Company’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions even where there is no regulated carbon price.
It acts as a shadow price by incentivizing emissions reductions and investment in low-carbon technologies, so that a higher carbon price promotes projects with lower emissions over the rest.

The internal carbon price in the scope of EU operations is established based on the current price and future forecasts for the European Union Emissions Trading Scheme (EU ETS), which is currently set at around $92 /t in 2026 and $110/t in 2030 in nominal terms.
This price is aligned with the EU ETS price path used by the Company in its financial statements to perform the asset impairment test.
For the rest of the world, in countries without more stringent specific regulation, an internal carbon price of $60/tCO2e is applied in the period 2026-2030.

This price applies mainly to Upstream projects and, when it refers to methane emission reduction and routine flaring projects, it especially facilitates investment, since the real cost of abatement is often less than $60/tCO2e.
Whenever an investment decision is made, the new project is analyzed with respect to the effect it could have on the Company’s direct emissions perimeter.
To this end, the internal carbon price is used in the valuation of any investment that may have an impact on the Company’s Scope 1 and 2 (which currently corresponds to a volume of 13.9 MtCO2e).
