Scenario selection: The C1 family of scenarios published in the IIASA (International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis) database has been taken as a reference, which groups the trajectories with a probability greater than 50% of limiting global warming to 1.5 °C by 2100, without exceeding this threshold or doing so in a limited way (≤ 0.1 °C) during a transitional period.
This family is distinguished from others (C2– C7) by its level of climate ambition and its compatibility with the objectives of the Paris Agreement.

The C1 scenarios include emissions from all sectors—energy, industry, transportation, AFOLU, and land use—and all primary sources (coal, oil, gas, nuclear, and renewables), allowing for a comprehensive analysis of global decarbonization.

Statistical treatment and reference analysis: From the set of 97 C1 scenarios, the median of the projected reduction of oil and gas emissions to 2030 has been calculated, considering this as the most robust trend measure against extreme values or asymmetric distributions.
Filtering Neutrality-Compatible Scenarios: Not all C1 scenarios are consistent with net zero in 2050.

Therefore, the subset of 19 C1 scenarios that achieve a reduction of more than 95% of emissions from the global energy system by 2050, considered compatible with net zero emissions trajectories, has been identified.

From this subset, a second filter has been applied to rule out assumptions considered technologically extreme or of low plausibility, including: • Scenarios with more than 1.5 Gt of CO₂ captured and stored (CCS) in 2030.
• Scenarios with high dependence on nuclear energy in the energy matrix.
• Scenarios with carbon prices above USD 750/tCO₂ in 2050, given their disproportionate impact on the economic and social viability of the transition.

The application of these filters has resulted in a final set of 9 representative scenarios, on which the median reduction in oil and gas emissions for 2030 has been recalculated, remaining around –14% compared to 2019.

Cross-validation and external consistency: As a verification exercise, the consistency of the results has been contrasted with the NZE (Net Zero Emissions by 2050) scenario prepared by the International Energy Agency (IEA), which is deterministic in nature and widely used as a sectoral reference.

This scenario, updated in the World Energy Outlook 2025, projects global reductions in oil and gas emissions of 22% and coal emissions of 32% by 2030 compared to 2019, reinforcing the consistency of the median obtained from the IPCC’s probabilistic scenarios.

Methodological conclusion: The methodology used combines scientific selection criteria (probability of meeting the 1.5 °C target), statistical consistency (median and interquartile intervals) and technological and economic plausibility (CCS technologies, nuclear and carbon prices).
This approach ensures that the comparison between corporate objectives and the trajectories of the IPCC is made on a scientifically grounded basis.

Absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions This metric includes: • Scope 1 and 2 (on an operational basis): Direct and indirect emissions from assets operated by the Company are accounted for following the GHG Protocol, considering the assets operated by the Company.
Repsol applies the operational control approach, including 100% of the emissions from the operations under its control, regardless of its shareholding or financial participation in such operations.
Emissions arising from non-operated Joint Operating Agreements (JOA) assets are not included and account for 0.7 MtCO2e in 2025.
• Scope 3, Category 11 (GHG Protocol): These are the emissions from the use of the fossil products sold.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M More precisely, the CII numerator includes: • Operational Scope 1 and 2: Direct and indirect emissions from Upstream, Refining and Chemical assets, as well as from the non-renewable electricity generation operated by Repsol.
For Scope 2 emissions, the market-based approach corresponding to the energy mix of the suppliers that supply steam and electricity is used.
• Scope 3: Emissions derived from the use of products that can be obtained from Repsol’s oil and gas production.
In the case of oil production, the yields in products are those of its own refining and chemical processes (category 11- upstream production based).
For the production of natural gas, all emissions resulting from its combustion (category 11) are counted.

In addition, emissions from third-party hydrogen plants supplying the Company's refineries (category 1- hydrogen purchases) are included, as well as emissions from final disposal after the use of chemicals (category 12).
• Carbon sinks: Emissions stored through carbon capture, use and storage managed by the Company.

Repsol’s decarbonization strategy does not rely on carbon offsets from natural-based solutions or carbon markets, except to address residual emissions once all available energy decarbonization options have been exhausted.
Carbon offsets have not been included in our decarbonization path for this decade and are only contemplated in the long term to offset residual emissions in order to reach carbon neutrality by 2050.
As for the denominator of the CII, it includes the following: • Energy products (Upstream production): It includes the energy contained in the production of oil and natural gas.
For oil, since we operate an integrated value chain, the energy of the products that can be obtained in our refining and chemical production scheme is included.
For chemicals and other non-energy products (such as lubricants and asphalt, among others), the energy contained in the oil used in their production is counted.

In the case of natural gas, given that we do not have an integrated value chain until final commercialization and that there is no net purchase of gas from third parties, the energy contained in the gas produced is included.

• Low-carbon energy sources: It includes energy produced from renewable electricity generation sources (solar, wind and hydro), energy from natural gas used in combined cycles and surplus cogeneration, as well as energy from renewable fuels (renewable hydrogen, biomethane, biofuels and synthetic fuels).

Renewable electrical energy is accounted for by the substitution method, as the equivalent amount of non-renewable energy needed to generate the same electricity in a conventional thermal plant, thus allowing consistent and comparable assessments of energy contributions.

For greater homogeneity of the financial information by business segments and non-financial segments, the calculation of both energy production and Scope 3 emissions is prepared using the same scope of entities and assets used by the Company, namely: • Companies/assets controlled by Repsol: 100% of energy production and Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are accounted for.
METHODOLOGY • Companies/assets under joint control: Energy production and Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are accounted for in proportion to Repsol’s percentage of ownership.

Interests in companies/assets in which the Company does not have control or joint control, constituting a mere financial interest: Energy production and Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are not counted.

Absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions The perimeter of this target follows the operational criteria established by the GHG Protocol44, which entails the inclusion of all business units, assets and operations in which Repsol has direct control over management and/or operational responsibility, accounting for 100% of the emissions generated.
Emissions are calculated using recognized methodologies, which are reported in Gross Scope 1, 2 and 3 and total GHG emissions (E1-6).

To reinforce the robustness and transparency of this data, Repsol collaborates with external organizations that provide methodologies and technical validation, such as Oil&Gas Methane Partnership (OGMP), which supports best practices in the measurement and reporting of methane emissions.
These external validations, together with international standards alignment, reinforce the operational excellence and credibility of the Company’s emissions reporting.
This metric is validated annually by an external auditor according to the ISO 14064 standard, covering more than 90% of Scope 1 and 2 inventory in this verification.

Methane intensity As most of the methane emissions from production operations come from natural gas production, this metric accounts for gas production in the denominator, while the numerator includes emissions associated with both gas and oil production.

Repsol’s methane emissions represent 4% of equivalent Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions, but its relevance lies on the operational excellence, which should aim to reduce methane losses to practically zero, with the consequent environmental and economic benefit.

Emissions are accounted for "from the wellhead to the point of sale," in line with the IEA’s outlook for natural gas, which includes oil and gas production, as well as natural gas gathering and processing.
The methane intensity metric is, in sum, the quotient between methane emissions and natural gas output at the point of sale, both expressed in volume and as a percentage.

Additionally, the numerator calculation (m3 CH4) complies with the OGMP methodology by implementing innovative quantification technologies that have been validated by UNEP, having awarded Repsol the Gold Standard.
44 The GHG Protocol guides for Scope 1, 2 and 3 can be found in Corporate Standard; Scope 2; Scope 3.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M METHODOLOGY 5.
Routine flaring emissions Metrics related to energy production Direct measurements of gas volume are made using flowmeters and sensors installed in flare systems in all operations.
The calculation of emissions is based on standardized formulas and factors, detailed in the Company’s internal emissions guidance.

For the segregation of emissions derived from routine operations and, therefore, likely to be reduced with more efficient operating strategies, the Company applies the definitions of the Global Flaring and Methane Reduction Partnership (GFMR) technical guide, which defines the different types of flaring in crude oil assets.
Energy consumption metrics The calculation of the energy consumed is obtained by multiplying the consumption of each fuel by its lower calorific value.
This comes from specific analyses of each fuel or from reference guides (IPCC, API and Concawe).
The bases for the calculation of energy and the scope are similar to those of section E1-6, where the origin of the emission sources and their calculation methodology are developed.

The energy consumed that is generated by third parties (Scope 2) is calculated from the purchase invoices and includes: • Steam consumed from outside: In the case of Repsol, it has fossil origins since it is generated in hydrogen production facilities by reforming natural gas or other hydrocarbons.
• Electricity consumed from outside: Depending on the marketer and the type of contract, this energy can be fossil, renewable or nuclear.

The consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling purchased or acquired from fossil sources corresponds to that indicated on the supplier's invoice, except for assets that consume electricity directly from the grid for which the electricity mix is used according to the geographical area.

Nuclear energy consumption corresponds to that indicated on the supplier’s invoice, except for assets that consume electricity directly from the grid for which the electricity mix is used according to the geographical area.

The consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling purchased or acquired from renewable sources corresponds to that indicated on the marketer’s bill, including the business units and assets that consume 100% renewable electricity, except for assets that consume electricity directly from the grid, for which the electricity mix is used according to the geographical area.
For all the cases described, the type of energy consumed is related to the supply contract of each asset.
This will depend on whether there are Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) or Guarantees of Origin (GoO) certificates.

If there are none, the residual or "remaining" emission factor (where the energy generated and sold specifically with GoOs is discounted) of the marketer (or of the country in the case of Direct Market Consumer assets) will be used.

The energy consumption data for non-operated JOAs is estimated from 2024 emissions using emission factors corresponding to the geographical areas where they are located and using the production ratio between 2025 and 2024.
Non-renewable energy production includes energy generated at Repsol's assets in the Industrial, Exploration and Production, and combined cycle power plant areas.
Renewable energy production comprises energy generated from wind farms, solar plants, hydroelectric power stations, and renewable fuels produced at industrial facilities.

Non-combustible renewable energy is expressed in terms of equivalent fossil energy, considering the average efficiency of a conventional thermal power plant to allow comparability with the Company’s other energy outputs.
GHG emissions Scope 1 and 2 emissions are those derived from operations and processes at the Company’s facilities.

These emissions can be classified into: Direct emissions (Scope 1) Those emitted directly at Repsol facilities, from the following sources: • By fuel consumption: The combustion reaction of any fuel results in heat, CO2, CH4, N2O, and water vapor.
They make up the bulk of Scope 1 emissions.
These emissions are calculated from direct measurements, such as fuel consumption and chemical composition, which means they can be accurately determined.
Combustion occurs in equipment such as engines, turbines, furnaces and boilers and other small-scale consumption such as pilot gas and purge gas in flares.
• By combustion of gas sent to flare.
• Process: Emissions from the process itself, characteristics of the Refining business such as, for example, the production of hydrogen from natural gas.
These are reported by direct and analytical measurement of the gas or by process unit balance.
• Vents: Emissions inherent in the equipment's design or operating practices, such as pneumatic devices or purges.
They are mainly methane emissions and are quantified by direct measurement.
• Fugitive: Fugitive emissions come from involuntary leaks in equipment used in operations.
This includes those from flanges, valve packings, compressor elements, pressure relief devices that vent to the atmosphere, etc.
They are mainly methane emissions and are quantified by direct measurement through Leak Detection & Repair (LDAR) campaigns.
Indirect emissions (Scope 2) Emissions from energy exchanges with third parties to or from Repsol facilities.
Emissions due to the import/export of steam and electric power are considered.

As it is energy exchanged with a third party, it is quantified through invoices, from which the emission factors of the marketing companies are also extracted and used to calculate the associated emissions.
In a manner similar to energy consumption, the residual mix emission factor of the marketer or the country in the case of Direct Market Consumers is used for this calculation.

The (location-based) Scope 2 methodology quantifies GHG emissions based on average emission factors of energy generation for given locations, including local, subnational or national borders (Annex I of Directive 2013/34/EU).
45 The substitution method is used by the USA Department of Energy, Science Based Targets initiative Energy Institute and the Our World in Data initiative which includes the University of Oxford.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M The (market-based) Scope 2 methodology quantifies the GHG emissions produced by generators from which the reporting entity purchases under contract with a marketer (Annex I of Directive 2013/34/EU).
Generally speaking, Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions are quantified following the guidelines of international standards widely recognized in the industry, such as IPCC, API, and Concawe.

Similarly, the breakdown at the emission source level and by business can only be accurately determined for emissions from operated assets as they are under the Company's control and the source and usage of each fuel is known.

In the specific case of methane (CH₄), the Company reports these emissions using advanced quantification methods, such as direct measurements or equivalent alternatives recognized by the OGMP 2.0 initiative.

As for the global warming factors for methane and N₂O, as of 2024, the IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) is used as a reference, which establishes a global warming factor (GWP) of 28.9 for methane and 273 for nitrous oxide (N₂O) over a 100- year time horizon, highlighting the greater climate impact of these gases compared to CO₂.
JOA’s non-operated Scope 1 and 2 emissions are estimated based on information provided by third parties in 2024 and updated using 2025 equity production.

Indirect emissions (Scope 3) The reported Scope 3 emissions are aligned with the principles established by the GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard and reflect the methodological approach followed to quantify the Company’s indirect value chain emissions.

After analyzing the entire emissions inventory for 2024, including the 15 Scope 3 categories, and considering that no significant changes have taken place that could have altered the materiality of these categories, the material categories and the corresponding calculation methods are detailed below.

A materiality threshold has been defined for those categories that account for more than 1.5% of total Scope 3 emissions, while ensuring that more than 95% of the emissions within this scope are always considered.
1: Purchased goods and services It includes purchases of crude oil, other main raw materials for Refining and Chemicals (hydrogen, ethylene, benzene, etc.
), purchases of biofuels and services contracted by the Company.

For the calculation of crude oil, biofuels and raw materials emissions, primary activity data from material balances are used together with emission factors from IOGP 2024 (International Association for Oil & Gas Producers) by regions and Ecoinvent 3.12.
In the case of hydrogen, an emission factor is used based on the European Union’s BREF Best Available Techniques (BAT) Reference Document for the Refining of Mineral Oil and Gas (JRC 2015).

The calculation of contracted services is based on the methodology spend-based using data from the Company’s economic purchasing activity and emission factors from the Carbon Footprint 2023 Evolution 2019-2023 document, May 2025.
Ministry for the Ecological Transition and the Demographic Challenge, in which the Ministry for the Environment calculates and publishes its own carbon footprint.

METHODOLOGY Category 11: Use of sold products This is calculated taking into account the sale of the Company’s petroleum products (Customer, Mobility Peru, Operators and Exports businesses) and net gas production by the Exploration and Production business.

To obtain category 11 emissions, the lower calorific value (GJ/t) and emission factor (tCO2/GJ) of each of the products is applied (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume 2 – Energy; Table 1.2 and 2.2).
All products are taken into account with the exception of chemical products, lubricants, asphalts and specialties, as they do not generate emissions in their use.
Biogenic emissions These emissions are related to the value chain of biogenic matter, both to feed Repsol processes and to be sold as biofuel.
Biogenic emissions associated with Scope 1 and 2 are not included as they are not material.
In the case of Scope 3, we distinguish: • Biogenic CO₂ emissions associated with the use phase of biofuels, calculated based on biofuel sales data and the required bio content in the marketed fuels.

For the calculation of CO2 emissions associated with the use of these fuels, the lower calorific value (GJ/t) according to the RED II directive and the emission factor (tCO2/GJ) of each of the products is applied (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume 2 – Energy; 2.2).
The result is 4.2 MtCO2 and it is not included in the results presented for Scope 3.

• Methane and N2O emissions associated with the biomass combustion use stage, calculated by applying the lower calorific value (GJ/t) according to the RED II directive and the corresponding emission factor for each GHG and product (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume – Energy; 2.2), with a result of 15 ktCO2e.

• Life cycle emissions of biomass not from combustion or degradation, already included in the results presented for Scope 3 and corresponding to the purchase of bio feedstocks with associated emissions of 1.1 MtCO2e and calculated with DEFRA factors.
Other considerations The methodology for calculating Scope 3, Category 11 emissions is based on the Company’s product sales.

The percentage of Scope 3 emissions calculated using primary data obtained from suppliers or other partners in the value chain is 91.7%, including data provided by flight travel agencies for category 6, category 15 emissions as data provided directly by the joint ventures, and considering category 11 emissions based on internal fuel sales data.

Methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O) are operationally relevant greenhouse gases, however, the detailed breakdown of these gases loses relevance for the quantification of Scope 3 emissions due to their materiality.
Despite this, most of the emission factors used to calculate Scope 3 emissions include both CH4 and N2O (DEFRA, IOGP, Ecoinvent).

Similarly, the breakdown at the emission source level and by business can only be accurately determined for emissions from operated assets as they are under the Company’s control and the source and usage of each fuel is known.
Scope 3 emissions, on the other hand, are indirect and therefore the only way to estimate them is by means of global emission factors in CO2e, which is why this breakdown is not available.

In this analysis, environmental experts from each business area have participated and the Company’s sites and businesses that are operated have been examined, excluding the rest of its own operations from the scope.

The amount of pollutants emitted, their dispersion into the environment, the legal limits established for emissions, the existence of pollution receptors and their vulnerability in relation to these pollutants were taken into account.

The following aspects have been identified as the IRO materials with a negative environmental impact in terms of pollution: • Emissions of pollutants generated in its own operations into water (IRO-14).
• Air emissions from both its production processes (IRO-15) and those generated by third parties due to the use of the fuel products offered by the Company (IRO-16).
• Emissions to the ground due to accidental releases or deviations from established procedures (IRO-17).

In addition to the double materiality assessment, a methodology has been developed to determine where the emissions of the substances that the Company considers a priority occur, as well as the business areas that contribute significantly to each IRO analyzed: • For air emissions, based on the volume of emissions recorded in Repsol’s internal environmental management tool, it has been identified that the Industrial and E&P businesses contribute significantly to the material IROs identified.

• For water emissions, based on local water-related risks, assessed with the Repsol Water Tool, weighted with respect to the relative volume of discharge from each facility, it has been identified that the Industrial business contributes significantly to the material IROs identified.
For more information on the Repsol Water Tool, see Impact, risk and opportunity management.

• For emissions to the soil, indirectly, given the technical difficulty to measure them due to their accidental nature, and based on the estimated costs to manage contaminated soils, it has been identified that the Industrial business and the Customer business contribute significantly to the material IROs identified.
In relation to air pollution, sulfur oxides (SO2), nitrogen oxides (NOx), volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and particles are identified as priority substances.
In water pollution, hydrocarbons, suspended solids and substances that cause an increase in chemical oxygen demand (COD) are a priority.

In soil contamination, in the event of an accidental release, priority is given to prevention by controlling all substances that may cause damage to health and the environment in case there is evidence of their presence, including hydrocarbons.

The Company also monitors other substances emitted in compliance with the requirements of applicable legislation, such as the European Industrial Emissions Directive (Integrated Pollution Prevention and Control E-PRTR).1 These assessments have not required consultations with potentially affected communities.

Policies related to pollution (E2-1) Repsol has embraced numerous commitments in its Environmental Policy, which governs its actions in all its activities and locations where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.

Specifically, the Company undertakes to prevent and control the impacts associated to air, water and soil emissions of pollutants derived from our operations and to reduce the potential environmental impact arising from the use of the products we manufacture, avoiding incidents and emergency situations.
The policy also promotes and requires environmental commitments in line with one’s own in the value chain, partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.
For more information, see Repsol’s policies related to sustainability.

• • It implements comprehensive management of discharges through advanced wastewater treatment systems, which include the segregation of effluents according to their level of contamination and the application of specific and effective treatments, with the aim of maximizing internal reuse and minimizing the pollutant load discharged.

It establishes internal safety standards for the design and operation of its activities, minimizing the risks of accidents that may lead to air, water or soil contamination due to the unintentional release of polluting substances.
2.2.2 Metrics and targets Targets related to pollution (E2-3) Repsol is making progress in the prevention and control of polluting emissions through the requirements of the applicable regulations.
This involves the adoption of the best available technologies (BAT) referenced by the European Union’s sectoral BREF3 documents.

In addition, Repsol has voluntarily set two targets4 to mitigate its material IROs in line with the commitments set out in the Environmental Policy regarding the prevention and control of impacts associated with emissions to the air, water, and soil of polluting substances derived from its operations.

3 The Best Available Techniques Reference (BREF) documents are the reference documents published by the European Union within the framework of Directive (EU) 2024/1785 on Industrial Emissions (IED), which include the best available technologies from the different industrial sectors and apply across Europe.
As further information to the targets described: 1.– Define action plans for the management and remediation of soil or groundwater in the event of contamination within less than 12 months.
In 2024, Repsol established this goal to prevent and control contamination of soil or groundwater, aligned with IROs 14 and 17 (as described in Materiality assessment).
Its definition is not based on the emission of specific pollutant loads.

By means of this target, the Company considers that the extent of contaminants present in the soil and/or groundwater is minimized, as is the potential impact that contaminants may have on existing receptors.
This target is monitored through the Company’s environmental situation reporting tool.
Progress toward achieving the 2025 target remains on track, according to the initial planning, and no significant deviations have been identified from the initially planned performance.
2.– Reduce emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) by 30% by 2035 compared with 2018 at the Refining and Chemical industrial complexes in the Iberian Peninsula.
In 2025, Repsol set this target for air pollution, in line with the IRO-15 (as described in Materiality assessment), which will first apply in 2026.
This target is not based on the emission of specific pollutant loads.

It is an absolute target linked to the decarbonization plan, the GHG emission reduction levers and the development of new production processes related to the use of renewable energy and renewable raw materials of the Industrial business.
In its definition, the projected reduction of NOx emissions achievable through the aforementioned levers has been estimated.
To this end, a correlation between CO2 emissions and NOx was used, based on historical emissions for both gases in the different facilities included within the scope of the target.
To monitor this target, the internal environmental parameter reporting tool will be used, from which NOx emissions data will be extracted annually.
In the case of water pollution, Repsol has not adopted specific targets beyond those established for water management, as defined in Water and marine resources.

Repsol Water Tool In addition to the double materiality assessment, an in- house tool called the Repsol Water Tool (RWT) has been used to identify and evaluate current and potential impacts, as well as material water-related risks and opportunities in the Company’s own operations.

The analysis conducted identified the businesses for which water is a material issue, namely the Refining and Chemicals business in the Iberian Peninsula and the Marcellus asset of the Upstream business in the USA.
Particular attention has been given to the amount of freshwater withdrawn from the environment, as this is considered to be the Company’s most significant impact in this topic.
For more details, see Methodology for water and marine resources reporting and metrics.
The results of the IRO assessment are taken into account when setting water-related policies and targets.
These, in turn, are aligned with the Company’s Strategic Plan for its transformation and an energy transition compatible with the global ambition to achieve net zero emissions.

Policies related to water management (E3-1) Repsol has established commitments in its Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all its activities and places where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.
These commitments give rise to the actions necessary to manage water-related environmental impacts.

The Company is focused on water management that includes the use and sourcing of water throughout the life cycle of its operations, products and services, and the long-term protection of available water resources, both terrestrial and marine.

Repsol will fulfill this commitment by increasing the use of sustainable supply from alternative water sources, including both internal and external reuse of water, thereby supporting the prevention and reduction of water pollution resulting from our activities through the treatment of effluents.
The Company has also set the ambition to reduce freshwater withdrawal from the environment, especially in areas at water risk.
These commitments are related to the material IROs associated with the reduction in the volume of available water resources, which is reported in this section.
The Environmental Policy includes in its scope all the Company’s facilities located in areas of high water stress and envisages practices related to the sustainability of the oceans and the seas.

The policy also pursues and requires environmental practices in the value chain that are in line with the Company’s own, including partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.
For more information, see Repsol’s policies related to sustainability.

2.3.2 Metrics and targets Water management targets (E3-3) Repsol has voluntarily defined two absolute5 targets in relation to water to address its material impacts, risks, and opportunities in line with the ambition set out in the Environmental Policy to reduce freshwater withdrawal from the environment, especially in water-stressed areas, in the vicinity of operations.
3 The time horizon has been modified from 2023-2025 to 2023-2026 because, once the pipeline has been installed, the plant has begun to reuse part of the discharge, which will continue in 2026.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration The scope of these targets encompasses operations that are material in the management of water-related IROs identified in the double materiality assessment.

Both targets take into account the improvement of water quality, since the reduction in freshwater withdrawal from the environment6 minimizes the impact on the conditions (quality and quantity) of the water available in ecosystems.
In addition, since withdrawal and consumption are two directly related variables, the aim is also to reduce the consumption of freshwater.

Water-related targets To define the targets, the use has been made of the methodology recommended by the CEO Water Mandate in the guide Setting Enterprise Water Targets , published in 2021 and developed by the World Resources Institute (WRI), Pacific Institute, CDP, The Nature Conservancy and WWF.
The indicator for tracking both targets is monitored using the Company’s environmental parameters reporting tool.

As an additional detail to the targets described: 1.– The target includes the following intermediate milestones: not increasing freshwater withdrawal by 2030 and reducing freshwater withdrawal by 30% by 2035.

2.– When defining this target, the following levers have been identified for its achievement: increasing water reuse up to 10% by 2030, reaching 5% by 2026, and regenerating the natural capital of the ecosystem in this area from 2035.

Analyzing the trend in net freshwater withdrawal from conventional environmental sources, it should be noted that in 2024 it stood at 39.02 Hm3 (-11.9% with respect to the base value) at the Refining and Chemicals business facilities in the Iberian Peninsula and 0.56 Hm3 (-67.1% with respect to the base value) at the Marcellus asset of the Upstream business.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M Methodology for water and marine resources reporting and metrics Repsol Water Tool The analysis methodology of the Repsol Water Tool (RWT) includes the following features: • The degree of measurement of the volume and quality of water flows in the facilities.
• The type of water (fresh and non-freshwater) and the volumes associated with each indicator (withdrawal, discharge, reuse).

• The quality of the water withdrawn, the existence of alternative sources of water supply and water treatment technologies (withdrawn, discharged and reused), the potential alteration of water quality (physical, chemical and biological parameters) downstream of the facility.

• The characterization of the withdrawal sources: type of source, type of water, location, impact of the withdrawal in relation to the average volume of the water body, degree of dependence on that source of supply, location of the source with respect to protected areas and key areas for biodiversity, presence of threatened species, importance of the water source for local communities and current and future level of water stress.
• The degree of competition for the resource and the future availability of water (guarantee of supply in the coming years, projections of future water demand).

For the identification of physical risks, the values of water stress (current and future) are taken into account both for the water withdrawal sources and for the points of discharge, as obtained from the Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas tool of the World Resource Institute (WRI).
To define the state of water bodies, the criteria established in the annexes of the European Directive 2000/60/EC (Water Framework Directive) have been used.
The input data entered into RWT is based on direct measurements and assessments made by those responsible for water management at each facility.
Water metrics The reporting criteria for water metrics are set out in a specific internal guide for the water vector.

It defines metrics and methodologies so that the reporting of these metrics complies with the legal requirements for sustainability reporting, supporting the homogeneity and traceability of the information throughout the Company.

Some analytical techniques reviewed are Standard Methods (SM) of the Examination of Water and Wastewater (from the APHA, AWWA, WPCF associations), series 2000 to 9000, Water Testing Standards (ASTM) and European Standards (EN) and standards published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO).

METHODOLOGY For the calculation of the metrics, the information is reported by category, according to the type of water (fresh or non-fresh), type of withdrawal source and type of effluent receiver, additionally indicating the use of water for the particular cases in which it is reused or recycled or, for example, if it is used for cooling processes in specific circuits.
This segregation, based on the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) water standard, allows the requested metrics to be calculated from the granularity of the Company’s water management.

The methodology used to calculate the metrics in Repsol’s operations under operational control establishes that 100% of the corresponding contribution is accounted for, regardless of the percentage of shareholding held by the Company, and is applied in accordance with the following criteria: • Water consumption: it is calculated as the difference between withdrawals and discharges, which includes both fresh and non-freshwater.

• Water consumption in areas with water risk: it is calculated by evaluating the level of water stress in the areas where both the withdrawal sources and the discharge points are located, using the Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas tool by World Resource Institute’s (WRI).
Regions with a high (40-80%) or very high (greater than 80%) percentage of water withdrawals are deemed to be areas at water risk.
This information relates to to areas where water has been identified as a matter of material importance.
• Recycled water: treated or untreated water that has been used more than once in the same process, before being discharged outside the Company’s boundaries.
• Reused water: treated or untreated water that has been used more than once in different processes of the Company or by a third party, before being discharged outside its limits.

• Stored water: water withdrawn from the environment that, without having been included in the report of the water consumption metric, is temporarily accumulated to enable water availability in operations and to be used when necessary for a certain operational purpose, whether present or future.
• Changes in water storage: the difference between the volume stored at the beginning and at the end of the reporting period.
• Water intensity: the ratio of total water consumption (m3) to net income.

In the specific case of water metrics, it is only possible to estimate consumption, since other indicators, such as the volume of water recycled, reused or stored, depend on the particular way that each asset is managed, which is conditioned by operational factors such as location, type of operation (onshore/offshore) and the nature of the asset (conventional/unconventional).

Water consumption was estimated based on production, applying ratios from assets operated under comparable conditions and, where no equivalent information was available, using average ratios from assets with similar characteristics.

Given that the estimate of water consumption in these assets represents less than 5% of the Company’s total, it has been deemed non-material and therefore excluded from the Company’s consolidated reporting.

None of them were deemed material in the double materiality assessment: • Potential risk of non-compliance with regulations related to biodiversity and the ecosystem protection in owned onshore operations or those located in sensitive marine environments, which may result in economic sanctions, suspension of activities, loss of licenses, reputational damage, and increased operating costs.

• Potential risk that onshore and offshore operations will be restricted, disrupted, or impeded in areas classified as sensitive or prioritized for biodiversity conservation, which could lead to delays in obtaining permits, project redesigns, or even to cancellation.
• The deterioration of coastal or river protective ecosystems may become an additional risk factor that could worsen potential damage to infrastructure in the event of heavy rainfall or waves.
• Loss of vegetation as a result of deforestation, construction activities and natural erosion may lead to a decrease in soil stability.
This can be an additional risk factor in exposure to landslides, which could lead to damage to infrastructure, increase operational downtime and raise repair costs.
Therefore, this assessment has identified transition and physical risks, and it has been determined that there are no systemic risks related to biodiversity and ecosystems that affect the Company.
• • • • Increased regulatory requirements that may result in greater difficulty in obtaining environmental authorizations for new projects or for renewing/ maintaining existing ones.
Increase in management costs (capex and opex) of impacts related to biodiversity and ecosystems.
The Company has adopted the scenarios set out in the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD)1 reporting framework as a reference for developing its long-term forecasts.

TNFD’s approach focuses on two critical uncertainties: 1) the deterioration of ecosystem services, which is correlated with physical risks, and 2) the alignment of market and non-market forces, which is associated with transition risks.
Four different scenarios are thus envisaged: • Ahead of the game: moderate deterioration of ecosystem services and high alignment of market and non-market forces.
• Back of the list: moderate deterioration of ecosystem services and low alignment of market and non-market forces.
For impact, risk and opportunities management, the Company undertakes commitments in its Environmental Policy and takes actions.

The Company is prepared for the most adverse scenarios thanks to its Strategic Plan, the decarbonization roadmap and the actions it carries out to manage its biodiversity- and ecosystem-related dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities.
Environmental experts from each business area have participated in this assessment and the Company’s operated sites and businesses have been examined, with other own operations left out of scope.

The following material negative impacts have been identified in relation to biodiversity and ecosystems, which are associated with the operations of the Industrial and LCG businesses: • Temporary loss of ecosystems and the services they provide as a result of the modification of the terrain and/or waterbeds, and the physical presence resulting from Repsol’s activities that affect a sensitive area, which may alter local flora and fauna (IRO-19).

The material positive impact identified is as follows: • Improvement of the conservation status of natural habitats and species populations, including those threatened, through the implementation of specific projects and nature-based solutions (NBSs) in the areas where the Company operates (IRO-20).

In addition to these material IROs, others have been identified that are related to other drivers of biodiversity loss (direct exploitation or climate change, for example) that can be consulted in climate change impact, risk and opportunity management and in water and marine resources impact, risk and opportunities management.
The double materiality assessment does not identify material negative impacts related to land deterioration, desertification or soil sealing.

LEAP Analysis Complementary to the double materiality assessment, a deeper understanding and identification has been achieved of the impacts and dependencies that result in different types of risks and opportunities for the Company with respect to nature, following TNFD’s LEAP approach.
For more details, see Methodology for biodiversity and ecosystems reporting and metrics.

To determine the latter, the Mean Species Abundance (MSA) has been used as an indicator, as modelled by GLOBIO4 and available on its website.

Its maximum value is calculated in the environment of each operation to which a semi-quantitative level has been attributed (Very low: <0,1; Low: 0.1-0.2; Moderate: 0.2-0.5; High: 0.5-0.7; Very high: 0,7-1).
The MSA is an indicator of the integrity of local biodiversity and represents the degree of alteration in the composition of species.

Repsol also accounts for the conservation and protection of biodiversity in the management of its own projects and operations by applying the mitigation hierarchy (avoid, minimize, restore, and compensate) during projects’ life cycle, especially in those located in protected areas.
Examples of these measures are reported in Actions and resources relating to biodiversity and ecosystems.

Repsol analyzes the potential presence of threatened species included in the Red List of the International Union for Conservation of Nature in the area of influence of its facilities using the Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) and has concluded that its operations may affect them.

Policies related to biodiversity and ecosystems (E4-2) Repsol has established commitments in its Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all the places where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.
These commitments give rise to the necessary actions to manage the impacts, dependencies, risks and material environmental opportunities related to biodiversity and ecosystems.

The Policy includes the following commitments that are related to material IROs of alteration of the presence and population size of species and temporary loss of terrestrial ecosystems and the services they provide as a result of the modification of terrain and/or waterbeds: • The sustainable use of resources.
• The protection and conservation of biodiversity, ecosystems and associated ecosystem services.
• Consideration of the different drivers of biodiversity loss, such as climate change, land/freshwater/sea use change, direct exploitation, the introduction of invasive species and pollution.

Therefore, the contribution to the direct drivers of biodiversity loss, impacts on the status of species, impacts on the extent and state of ecosystems, and impacts and dependencies on ecosystem services have been considered when establishing the commitments of the Policy and the Company’s management must take them into account when they are material.
In this way, Repsol seeks to contribute to goals 1, 4, 6, 7, 8 and 15 of the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), given the importance that Repsol attributes to this framework.
The Policy prioritizes the application of the mitigation hierarchy, which entails the use of ecosystem restoration as a practice to manage deforestation.
Examples can be found in Actions and resources relating to biodiversity and ecosystems.

The same policy has also made commitments in relation to the sustainability of the oceans and seas and promotes the sustainable sourcing and use of resources, although it does not explicitly address production, procurement or consumption from ecosystems managed to maintain or improve conditions for biodiversity, since most of the input resources to the Company are technical materials.
For more details, see Resource use and circular economy.

The policy also promotes and requires environmental commitments in the value chain that are in line with Repsol’s own commitments, including partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.
The consequences of impacts related to biodiversity and ecosystems on affected communities are addressed following the guidelines established in the Human Rights and Community Relations Policy.

Specifically, those associated with the biodiversity loss due to land, sea, or freshwater use change, or the alteration of the presence and population size of species are included.
Actions related to activities whose impact on biodiversity is due to other drivers are reported in Climate change and Water and marine resources.

These objectives are aligned with the commitments established in the Environmental Policy to protect and conserve biodiversity, ecosystems and Biodiversity and ecosystem-related objectives the associated ecosystem services, managing the impacts and dependencies on them and the communities and stakeholders that may be affected throughout the life cycle of operations, products and services, with special attention focused on impacts that take place within or near protected areas.
Further details on the targets described: 1.– Improve the conservation status of endangered species.
The Company undertakes conservation projects to minimize, restore and compensate for the magnitude of possible direct impacts on threatened species, improving their populations and habitats.

This objective, as part of the Company’s voluntary goals, supports the Global Biodiversity Framework (objective 4), which seeks to reduce the risk of extinction of threatened species through their recovery and conservation, although it does not entail external validation.
No ecological thresholds have been applied when setting the target, the first year of which will be 2026.
2.– Adopt action plans for biodiversity in assets located in sensitive areas for biodiversity.

Beyond the commitment defined by the Company years ago to observe voluntary exclusion areas9 , Repsol has established a relative (%) and continuous target in 2025 related to the management of impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems in sensitive areas.
The Company believes that it avoids, minimizes, restores and compensates for the magnitude of the possible impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems through the actions associated with this target.
Repsol will consider as equivalent plans different biodiversity impact management measures such as monitoring, surveillance plans, the implementation of complementary impact measures, etc.

To remedy this deficiency, the 2025 report aims to break down the information on surface areas of each of the individual concession blocks making up to total in each county.
In this LEAP analysis, it has not been necessary to carry out consultations with potentially affected communities.
The first component of the LEAP assessment (L for Locate ) involves creating a list of assets located in sensitive areas or with potentially material impacts or dependencies.

Each of the activities has been evaluated using tools such as ENCORE13, and guides such as those published by the World Business Council for Sustainable Development14, which collect general information by type of activity.

Likewise, the activities have been analyzed according to their location, based on geo-referenced information, identifying biomes of importance for biodiversity and interaction with sensitive areas for nature.
According to TNFD, a site is sensitive to nature if it impacts on: • Areas of importance for biodiversity.
• Areas of high ecosystem integrity.
• Areas with a high water risk.
• Areas of importance for the provision of ecosystem services, including benefits to local and indigenous communities and other stakeholders.
The second evaluation component of the LEAP approach (E for Evaluate) allows for the measurement of dependencies and impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems.
The ENCORE tool has been used for the dependencies, attributing a semi-quantitative level to them for each ecosystem service.

For impacts, the Company has developed the Reads15 tool, which can be used to assess the environmental impacts of projects and activities during the construction, operation and decommissioning stages.
Reads enables users to understand the effects of a project or activity on climate, water resources, ecosystem services and social well-being.

Based on these assessments, Reads calculates an indicator of impact on biodiversity taking into account the different drivers of biodiversity loss: land use change, greenhouse gas emissions, direct exploitation of natural resources, and pollution.
In addition, it can go beyond sectoral averages by incorporating site-level data to assess environmental impacts.
In recognition of the importance of the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), Repsol seeks to contribute to the GBF’s 2030 and 2050 goals through its efforts.

Thus, for example, the Company includes in this report (see table of Sites with a material impact on biodiversity and ecosystems) the impacts and dependencies on biodiversity and ecosystems, in line with Target 15 of the Global Biodiversity Framework.
Finally, the third evaluation component of the LEAP approach (A stands for Assess) aims to identify and assess nature-related risks and opportunities.
An identification of the typology of risks collected by TNFD has been carried out, which includes physical risks, related to dependencies, and transition risks, associated with the impacts.
It has been determined that there are no systemic nature-related risks affecting the Company.
The Company has been making progress over the past two years in the application of LEAP analysis, and intends to continue advancing and make it more robust year by year.

To this end, throughout this year, progress has been made in the number and types of assets modelled in Reads, as well as in the frequency of updating the data to have the most up-to-date quantification of impacts possible; the dependency analysis has been updated with the new version of ENCORE; the measurement of the main habitats where the Company's sites are located following the IUCN classification scheme has been incorporated; etc.
For example, ecosystem condition data are not available for offshore sites.

This information makes it possible to determine that the contribution to the material IROs occurs in the Upstream and Industrial businesses, in which: • There is an influx of raw materials in the form of mineral hydrocarbons.
• The mineral hydrocarbons used can be replaced by other raw materials of renewable origin or derived from waste.

The following material IROs have been identified in the field of resource use and circular economy: • As a positive impact, the development of circular or bio-based products and renewable fuels from renewable and recycled secondary raw materials, as it reduces the adverse effects associated with the disposal of waste generated by society (IRO-22).
• As a negative impact, the extraction of non-renewable resources from nature, which contributes to the reduction of the world’s reserves of them (IRO-23).
• As a negative impact, the offering of products made with non-renewable nor recycled materials (IRO-24).

In addition to the double materiality assessment, own operations have been identified in which non-renewable resources are used to a greater extent and in which there are products and services for which circular economy criteria can be applied.
To this end, the quantities and types of resources that enter the activities and the circularity characteristics of the different families of manufactured products have been analyzed.

Policies related to resource use and the circular economy (E5-1) Repsol has established commitments in its Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all its activities and places where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.
The necessary actions to manage the material environmental impacts related to the use of resources and the circular economy arise from these goals.

In particular, Repsol is focused on integrating the principles of the circular economy into its processes, products, and services, optimizing the use of resources and raw materials, including energy and water; fostering the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained in a circular manner; and promoting the development of new products with less environmental impact.

In addition, it is aligned with the industrial transformation that Repsol is carrying out and could imply a relative reduction in the use of virgin and non-renewable resources, thanks to the fact that a portion1 of the demand for Repsol’s products will be met with products manufactured from renewable and/or non-virgin raw materials.

The policy also promotes and requires environmental commitments in the value chain in line with the Company’s own, including partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.

Actions and resources related to resource use and the circular economy (E5-2) The following tables provide information on the actions taken and planned by Repsol related to the use of resources and the circular economy.

Its implementation contributes to the achievement of the commitments established in the Environmental Policy, specifically to the integration of the principles of the circular economy in its processes, products and services, the optimization of the use of resources and raw materials, the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained in a sustainable way; and the development of new products with less impact on the environment.

Specifically, actions 1.1 and 1.2 contribute to the target of reaching a production volume of between 125,000 and 146,000 tons by 2030 of circular or bio-based products that incorporate alternative raw materials such as waste plastic or other renewable materials.

2.5.2 Metrics and targets Targets related to resource use and the circular economy (E5-3) In 2024, Repsol updated its Strategic Plan for 2024-2027, defining several voluntary objectives2 to manage its impacts, risks, and material opportunities in line with the commitments set out in the Environmental Policy, namely: to integrate the principles of the circular economy into processes, products, and services; optimize the use of resources and raw materials; optimize the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained circularly; and develop new products with less impact on the environment.

These targets, which are of an absolute nature, are aligned with IRO-22, as described in Materiality assessment, and relate to both the inflow and outflow of resources, encouraging the use of circular materials, the minimization of primary raw materials, and the circular sourcing and use of renewable resources.
The calculation and tracking of these indicators is monitored through the project management tool of the Industrial business.
Its achievement serves as a lever for the reduction of the Company’s CO2 emissions in all its scopes.

Repsol has reviewed the objectives and eliminated intermediate milestones to adapt them to the global energy context, changes in regulation and demand, and the pace of the energy transition, without ceasing to make progress in achieving the commitments in the Environmental Policy, while making investments that are profitable in the new environment.
(2) Own operations industrial business in which circular or bio-based products are produced.
(3) Own operations to the Industrial business in which biomethane is produced.
Further details on the targets described: 1.– Reach a renewable fuel production capacity of between 1.6 and 1.8 million tons in the Iberian Peninsula by 2030.
Renewable fuels are produced from sustainable sourced waste raw materials or from the capture of CO2 emissions.
This target has been set with reference to a renewable fuel production capacity value of 1 million tons in 2023.
It includes co-processing, ETBE, and renewable H2 as intermediates.

The objective originally defined in the 2024-2027 Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a production capacity of renewable fuels in the Iberian Peninsula of more than 2.2 million tons by 2030.
The production capacity of renewable fuels stood at 1.25 million tons at the end of 2025 (1.2 million tons in 2024).

2.- Reach a production volume of between 125,000 and 146,000 tons by 2030 of circular or biological origin products incorporating alternative raw materials such as waste plastic or other renewable materials.

Repsol’s circular or biological origin products include alternative raw materials, such as plastic waste or renewable raw materials, where renewable fuels and biomethane do not count toward this objective.

This objective has been defined with respect to a reference value for the production of circular or biological origin products of 9,372 tons in 2023 and account is taken of changes in regulations, technology and markets associated with circular or biological origin products in the definition.

The objective originally defined in the 2024-2027 Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a production capacity of circular or biological origin products incorporating alternative raw materials such as waste plastic or other renewable materials of more than 150,000 tons by 2030.
The target, which was initially communicated as circular or bio-based products, is adapted to the terminology in line with the current state of reference standards.

These products are obtained through mechanical recycling, which includes a percentage of recycled material content certified by RecyClass; chemical recycling, which includes a percentage of recycled material content, allocated by mass balance and certified by ISCC Plus; or recycling of bio-based materials, which includes a percentage of renewable or bio material content, assigned by mass balance and certified by ISCC EU (Renewable Energy Directive 2018/2001).

The production of circular and biological-based products came to 25,137 tons at the end of 2025 (12,227 tons in 2024).
3.- Reach a biomethane production capacity in 2030, from organic waste, of between 0.7 and 0.8 TWh.
Biomethane is a renewable alternative to natural gas that originates from organic matter, such as agricultural and livestock waste.

In addition to its use as a substitute for conventional gas, it has other industrial applications, such as the production of renewable fuels and renewable hydrogen or the manufacture of chemical products, with an associated reduction in greenhouse gases.
This target has been set against a biomethane production reference value of 0 TWh in 2023.
The target originally defined in the 2024-2027 Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a production capacity of biomethane from organic waste of more than 2.1 TWh by 2030.

2.6 Taxonomy Regulation As explained in Climate change, Repsol’s investments that directly contribute to meeting its decarbonization goals are called low-carbon investments and include energy efficiency, generation and commercialization of renewable electricity, production and commercialization of renewable fuels, renewable hydrogen, synthetic fuels, chemicals (long-life polymers), other circular economy projects, carbon capture and storage (CCS), distributed generation, renewable electric mobility, and investments in R&D and corporate venturing in low- carbon technologies.

This vision of the decarbonization of our activity is less restrictive1 than that laid down by the European Union in its framework for facilitating sustainable investments, called the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy.

The taxonomy defines a series of "eligible" economic activities, which are encompassed in 16 macro-sectors, and establishes the technical selection criteria to determine whether, if eligible, they are "aligned" activities: that is, those that contribute substantially to at least one of the six environmental objectives defined by the EU, do not pose significant damage to the other objectives and also comply with a minimum of social safeguards.

In 2025, the percentage of activities aligned according to the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy out of the Group’s total has risen to a percentage of 28% capex (40% in 2024), 0.8% of revenues (0.8% in 2024) and of 5% opex (4% in 2024).

Processes for engaging with value chain workers (S2-2) Collaboration with suppliers and contractors Repsol manages its supply chain by responding to both the expectations of stakeholders and complying with ethical, labor, environmental, safety and human rights standards.
In this process, social, environmental and business conduct aspects, among others, are evaluated.

In the event that risks or aspects for improvement related to social, environmental, or governance issues have been identified in the due diligence process, Repsol has the prerogative of introducing corrective or commitment clauses in the Shareholders' Agreement to achieve the necessary standards, along with the contractual mechanism, in order to verify corrective measures or commitments subsequent to the signing of the contract.

Managing negative impacts in the supply chain Repsol has a supply chain risk management model to identify, assess, and minimize environmental, social, and governance (ESG) risks at different stages of the purchasing process.

Managing supply chain risk 12 The Gas Trading and Wholesale businesses have integrated compliance departments, which have autonomy in decision-making regarding the due diligence of the third parties involved in their operations.

In the purchase and contracting phase, through specific contractual clauses, the Company requires a compliance with international standards, and provisions relating to safety, the environment, data protection, information security, cybersecurity, ethical conduct and respect for human rights in its internal regulations.

During the bidding process, in acquisitions of goods and services of high risk in safety and environment, it is required to introduce a minimum weight in the weighting criteria of the matrix of analysis, evaluation, comparison and alignment of bids, with a minimum acceptable value required.
In the tender process, safety and environmental reference persons evaluate suppliers in significant aspects of the new contract.

In addition, in the Upstream business, a target for 2026 has been defined that perfects this process and is described in more detail in Targets related to managing material negative impacts, driving positive impacts, and managing material risks and opportunities (S2-5).

Supporting the effective implementation of the impact management process There is a process of periodic evaluation of the performance of suppliers in terms of safety, environment, quality and management in order to identify risk situations that may require corrective actions, termination or suspension of the relationship.
In the case of goods and services with a high risk in safety and the environment, an action plan is drawn up jointly with the supplier if the evaluation falls short of a certain threshold.
In 2025, no provider was disqualified for ESG reasons.
There were no severe impacts on human rights, nor breaches of contract due to social or environmental impacts in the year related to the previous and subsequent phases of its value chain.

• Safety and Environment function: Responsible for defining which activities and purchases are of high risk in safety and the environment, as well as evaluating the risk and other significant aspects of suppliers prior to contracting.

Training and awareness for those involved in purchasing processes In order to promote ESG training for employees in the Value Chain (VC), Repsol has collaborated with the Spanish Network of the United Nations Global Compact to boost the participation of small and medium-sized companies in its global supply chain in a training program.

The training itinerary covers general aspects of sustainability, SDGs, the 10 Principles of the Global Compact (human rights, labor standards, environment and the fight against corruption), communication and sustainability reporting.
Likewise, objectives are established annually to train employees in the Purchasing department in the areas of ethics and conduct, cybersecurity risks, and other pertinent sustainability topics.

In industrial complexes, LPG factories and renewable generation assets, mandatory training is provided on the risks of the facilities where workers are employed, regardless of whether they are company personnel or not.

The time horizon tied to this action is not limited, given that it is integral to the ESG strategy Positive impact on the supply chain Repsol promotes the participation of local companies in its network of suppliers and contractors and, in doing so, favors the generation of employment and the protection of workers’ rights in local communities, improving economic and social development in the areas in which it operates.

This committee promotes and supervises the commitments contained in the Sustainability Plan and the Strategic Plan, aimed at guaranteeing a work environment that reflects the diversity of the society in which the Company operates.

Safety and Occupational Health Policy Goal To carry out all the Company’s activities preserving the integrity of people and avoiding any damage to the surroundings and the environment, as well as to ensure a healthy and safe work environment from both a physical and emotional point of view.
Risk management will also focus on avoiding any damage to people and the environment, in particular the prevention of major accidents.

Environmental Policy Goal To provide energy and products to society to meet its needs efficiently, guaranteeing the adequate protection of the environment and the sustainable use of resources without compromising the well- being of future generations.

The commitments contained in this policy are articulated as a fundamental lever to achieve the Company’s goal of being net zero emissions by 2050, in line with the objectives of the Paris Agreement to combat climate change, as well as with the objective of promoting environmental protection and the conservation of natural capital.
Description Repsol is committed to protecting the environment, the sustainable use of resources, and the prevention of pollution.

Therefore, priority is given to the actions necessary to identify, evaluate and manage, following the hierarchy of mitigation 1, our impacts, dependencies, risks and opportunities on the environment, in our activities and the places where we operate, along the value chain, as well as in the products and services we offer to society.

To this end, measures related to mitigation and adaptation to climate change, the integration of the principles of the circular economy, the prevention of waste generation, the protection and conservation of biodiversity, ecosystems and ecosystem services, water management throughout the life cycle of operations, and products and services, and the prevention of impacts associated with emissions to air, water, and soil of substances polluting the air, water, and soil, derived from Repsol’s operations.

Sustainability Policy Goal Date of approval/revision Description To meet the growing demand for energy and products by maximizing Repsol’s contribution to sustainable development, to meet present needs without compromising those of future generations, with the commitment to become a net-zero emissions company by 2050 in line with the objectives of the Paris Agreement.

This policy includes the commitment to support the United Nations 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and its Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) as well as the principles of the Global Compact in terms of human rights, labor standards, the environment and the fight against corruption.
It also promotes a fair energy transition that takes into account its impact on workers, local communities and society in general.

To this end, Repsol is committed to integrating sustainability into all the Company’s businesses and organizational levels and to promote and require sustainability commitments in accordance with/similar to those defined internally throughout its value chain: customers, partners, suppliers of products and services and other stakeholders.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration 5.2 Taxonomy Regulation On June 18, 2020, the European Parliament adopted Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on the establishment of a framework to facilitate sustainable investments (also known as the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy and hereinafter referred to as “SFT”).

This taxonomy, which entered into force in 2021, defines a number of economic activities ("eligible" activities), which fall under 16 macro-sectors, and sets out the technical selection criteria for determining whether they make a substantial contribution to climate change mitigation and adaptation objectives.

For an "eligible" activity to be considered environmentally sustainable, and therefore "aligned" with the taxonomy, it must make a substantial contribution to at least one of the six environmental objectives defined by the EU, not cause significant damage to the remaining objectives, and also comply with a minimum number of social safeguards.

Alignment determination process The process of determining the alignment of the activities identified as "eligible" by the SFT begins by verifying compliance with the criteria for making a substantial contribution to one of the six defined environmental objectives.

Once the activities that meet the requirements are identified, compliance with the criteria of do no significant harm ("DNSH") is validated with respect to the different environmental objectives (adaptation to climate change, water resources, pollution, circular economy, and biodiversity).
Compliance with the criterion of making a substantial contribution to climate change mitigation The taxonomy establishes different types of substantial contribution criteria.

• Activities that must exceed an established threshold of GHG emissions savings throughout their life cycle when compared to the conventional process or generate GHG emissions below a certain threshold.
• Activities that must exceed a certain energy density threshold.

Repsol’s activities are framed within the energy sector; therefore, after analyzing the Company’s contribution to the six environmental objectives established in the Delegated Regulation, the conclusion was reached that, at present, the only material objective to report is the mitigation of climate change.

While none of the Company’s activities are directly linked to the other five environmental objectives, certain complementary activities such as water treatment or waste management could be considered eligible.
