(5 )It includes projects for the development, construction and operation of solar parks for the generation of electricity from photovoltaic solar technology.
(6) It includes projects for the development, construction and operation of wind farms for electricity generation from wind energy.
(7) It includes projects for the operation of hydroelectric power plants for the generation of electricity.
(8) It includes the development, construction and operation of pumped hydroelectric plants.
(9) It includes projects for the development and construction of renewable fuels (hydrobiodiesel, biojet, biogasoline and biopropane) for aircraft, ships, trucks or cars.
(10) It includes the operation of combined cycle plants for the generation of electricity from gaseous fossil fuels.
(11) It includes the operation of the cogeneration systems located in the combined cycle plants for the generation of electricity and heat from gaseous fossil fuels.
(12) Projects in the exploratory phase or in the initial stages of development for the permanent geological storage of CO2, including transport from the point of capture.
These include both the dimensioning and characterization of possible geological repositories, as well as the associated initial engineering activities.
(13) It includes the installation of electric charging points at service stations (14) It includes the installation of electric charging points in building parking lots.

(15) It includes the installation of solar panels in the different installations: LPG factories, refineries, chemical complexes, etc., as well as with third parties through initiatives such as Solmatch.
(16) It includes digital projects for monitoring and reducing GHG emissions.
(17) It includes research, development and innovation projects of technologies for the manufacture of aggregates through the capture of CO2.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Types of activities Depending on the contribution that an activity makes to the different objectives, it can be classified as follows: • Activities that, by their own performance, contribute to stabilizing greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere.
In the case of Repsol, this would correspond to the activities of renewable electricity generation or biofuel manufacturing, among others.
• Enabling: When the activity allows the performance of other sustainable activities, such as, for example, the installation, maintenance and repair of renewable energy technologies.
• Transitional: When the activity supports the transition to a climate-neutral economy, such as the manufacture of plastics in primary forms.

Repsol has developed a semi-quantitative methodology to analyze in detail the physical risks of climate change at existing facilities, and especially at new facilities that have been added to the Company’s portfolio.

To carry out this long-term analysis, the global warming scenarios described by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) have been used, with a wide range of emissions (SSP5-8.5, SSP2-4.5 and SSP1-2.6 scenarios) selected with time horizons of 2030, 2040, and 2050.
At the moment, the physical risk assessment work shows a low risk in general, due to the engineering design bases of these facilities and the adaptation measures implemented.
See Climate change for more details.
• Sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources.

The Company has environmental impact studies, which include assessments of impacts on the hydric environment, and reports on the ecological, chemical and physical status of the waters, so that the ecological quality of water flows is at levels aligned with the Water Framework Directive to which the DNSH criterion refers.
• Transition to a circular economy.

In 2024, Repsol approved its Environmental Policy, which establishes specific commitments in the field of circular economy: promoting the application of the principles of the circular economy and optimizing the use of natural resources and raw materials, including energy and water resources.
In addition, since 2019 the Group has had a framework contract with the company Surus for the application of circular economy principles to all the assets that are no longer used by the Company.
Repsol participates in international consortia that promote circular economy principles.
What’s more, the circular economy commitments are applied in each project through the contracting of suppliers that have express commitments to withdraw and reuse equipment and components.
Energy storage, Repsol has implemented a waste management plan aimed at ensuring the highest possible level of recycling of the waste generated by this activity.
• Pollution control and prevention.
Repsol has specific reports on the implementation of the Best Available Techniques (BAT) and on compliance with the emissions/discharge limits associated therewith.
• Protection and recovery of biodiversity and ecosystems.

The Company has environmental impact studies and reports on mitigation and compensation measures adopted in the affected terrestrial ecosystems, which guarantees compliance with DNSH principles in this area.

Information on society a) Commitment to sustainable development: partnership and sponsorship actions Repsol actively participates in various global initiatives and associations related to sustainability, as well as in other thematic initiatives and associations related to different ESG aspects.
These collaborations allow Repsol and other companies to share best practices, and learn from other organizations, fostering continuous improvement and progress in sustainability.
In addition, these collaborations are established as one of the 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the United Nations.

These are some of these associations and initiatives in which Repsol participates: • Spanish Network of the United Nations Global Compact • IPIECA • OGCI • Zero Routine Flaring by 2030 (World Bank) • Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAP) • Natural Capital Coalition • CEO Water Mandate • International Process Safety Group (IPSG) • Concawe • Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative (EITI) • Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights b) Subcontracting and suppliers [GRI 308-1] New suppliers that were screened using environmental criteria.

[414-1] New suppliers that were screened using social criteria In 2025, as in 2024, 100% of new suppliers were screened based on criteria related to human rights, social, labor, and environmental criteria.

[GRI 308-2] Negative environmental impacts in the supply chain and actions taken In 2025, a total of 1,949 environmental assessments (2,381 in 2024) were carried out on 1,204 suppliers (1,569 in 2024).
In 25 evaluations, corresponding to 21 suppliers, the environmental performance rating was less than 5 out of 10 (24 evaluations corresponding to 20 suppliers in 2024).

[GRI 414-2] Negative social impacts in the supply chain and actions taken In 2025, as in 2024, there was no negative social impact on the Company’s suppliers in the supply chain, so it was not necessary to define any action.

In addition, in 2025, funds were received from public authorities to finance the development of two electrolyzers at the Cartagena and Bilbao refineries to produce renewable hydrogen in the amount of €315 million (see Note 19 of the Consolidated Financial Statements).

The SNFI has been drawn up in accordance with the provisions of current mercantile legislation and in accordance with the selected criteria contained in Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2772 of 31 July 2023, regarding sustainability reporting standards (i.e., the ESRS), as well as other criteria described for each topic as specified in table 5.4.1 'Non-financial information and diversity required by Law 11/2018', which forms part of Annex IV 'Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information' of the attached Consolidated Management Report.

Inherent limitations in preparing the information In accordance with certain ESRS selected by Repsol to prepare its SNFI, the Parent company's directors are required to prepare prospective information based on assumptions and hypotheses, which should be included in the sustainability information, regarding events that could occur in the future, as well as possible future actions, where appropriate, that the Group could take.

In determining disclosures relating to sustainability information, the Parent company's directors interpret legal and other terms that are not clearly defined and could be interpreted differently by others, including the legality of such interpretations and, consequently, they are subject to uncertainty.
The engagement team consisted of professionals specialising in Non-financial Information reviews, specifically in information on economic, social and environmental performance.
